She frequently throws in funny anecdotes or relevant medical applications. Labs force you to think and group quizzes are brutal, but compared to 2070, the class is much less demanding.

Wow, what a course. This class is VERY hard, especially if you have a limited chem background like myself. That being said, it is not impossible if you utilize every resource possible. Hines is amazing, helpful, and super kind. Get ready to work hard and take a lighter schedule with this class. The problem sets can be RIDIC

Hines is a great professor! She is super nice and actually cares about the students. She understands that most kids in her class are biopremed students so she did a lot of examples relating to chemistry applications in medicine. This wasn't an easy class, but she wasn't out there to get you like other professors sometimes try to do.

One of the best professors I've had! Great lecturer that gives real life examples based on materials being learned in class. Very generous with exams and curve. Very helpful when you go to office hours. Bad points: group quizzes that TAs are not allowed to help on. If you're stuck with a stupid group and a harsh TA, you're out of luck.

Hines is a great lecturer I believe who explains everything very well. I opened the textbook once or twice throughout the semester; she is clear on what you should know. I only hated having group quizzes, but if you can do them mostly then you'll be fine for prelims. Learning catalytics is very helpful and good to review 4 prelims. Curves generous.
Good professor, class is challenging but curve is generous. Group quizzes can be hit or miss depending on your group especially since TA's won't help you.

Favorite Professor of Freshman Year! Lectures are incredibly clear and loved learning catalytics test questions. The Problem Sets are extremely hard and the lab is tough but thought her tests were very fair.

Hines obviously wants her students to succeed, and her lectures are helpful and fairly easy to follow. The prob sets (aka group quizzes) were usually very hard, and the labs were challenging. The group quizzes can bring your grade up, but if you're too embarrassed to ask the strangers in your group how they did a prob, you can get left behind.

The class seemed a lot like a high school class. Tests are very predictable, homework and labs are easy. It's very clear what she expects you to know. She moves at a slow pace and practically screams her lectures. Having to turn in homework in groups is the worst idea ever.

She clearly tries really hard in lecture and knows the material, but the administrative changes she's enacted (like forbidding help with homework and labs, even from TAs) is incredibly obnoxious. Depending on your TA or assigned study group, you can really get screwed. Prelims are usually difficult and learning catalytics is unnecessary.

She is an excellent professor who tries really hard to keep you interested during thee lectures. Overall, I like her philosophy on teaching and her teaching style. The quizzes can be challenging however and this may just be my TA, but I've found that the labs are graded more harshly than in 2090.
Her lectures are one of the best at Cornell. Very clear and well organized. Problems sets are hard. you must go to office hours for help. The Prelims have low means. good class overall

Difficult course, but her lectures are really good. Always attend, copying someone else's notes doesn't really help in this course. Expect to spend a long time in OH for the problem sets, and have some pretty low means on the prelims. Overall a good course though.

Was my first course on quantum mechanics and absolutely loved the class. Lecture attendance crucial to understanding the material; found exams to be very straightforward if concepts learned in class were understood. Problem sets are do-able but may require a couple hours to complete.

The queen of technology!  Her problemsets are impeccably done, but are fairly difficult.  If you get stuck with a crappy group, then you`re pretty much screwed (like I was), thereby bringing down your grade sadly.  Overall, I learned a lot though.

After taking Chem 390 this past semester, I realize just how lucky I was to have professor hines teach 389:  quantum mech is hard but her tests were doable and lecture notes were extremely clear and useful in preparing for the exams! Good professor..

QM is a daunting subject, but in the hands of a lecturer as capable as prof Hines, it's totally doable.  She was a fantastic lecturer, but definitely take linear algebra either before or during this course.  That saved me more than once.  Class is also geared towards Chem majors rather than ChemE's, and that was fine with me.

Prof Hines obv knows what she is talking about...but she moves too quickly.  Going to office hours is terrifying.  She is not approachable or friendly.  As many of us observed, she doesn't seem as helpful to guys in the class as she is to girls...seemed like she never went the extra mile for guys, but if you're a girl no prob. it was irritating.
Professor Hines made Chem209 surprisingly bearable. Clear lectures and very reasonable exams. Problem sets are time consuming, however.

Although this course was tough (NO ONE wants to take it), we lucked out by not getting Freed. Although Hines' lectures move at a fast pace, she teaches the material well and answers students' questions when asked. The textbook she chose (Metiu) is worthless. Also, learn how to use Mathematica: you won't be able to do the problem sets without it.

difficult course - def harder than 208. makes you work hard but if you put the work in you'll do well. problems sets are really hard, and the prelims are not pieces of cake either, you gotta know your stuff
all i can say is don't enter her dungeon(office) or you may not pee right for a week.  i dont know what that means,  but i thought she was mean and unhelpful and scary.
Very clear and organized lectures.  Hard problem sets but comparably easier prelims!

Very smart professor! Gives really challenging but interesting problem sets.  After answering the problem sets correctly you get a sense of confidence.  The prelims are not as hard as the problem sets.
I hated this class so much, but once i got to know hines better, she's a really nice woman

I'm a senior now but I took Chem 208 as a freshman and Hines was seriously one of two of my favorite science profs at Cornell. Yes, she goes fast and yes, the problem sets are a pain especially if your group consists of a bunch of slackers. But her notes are amazingly clear and no one ever said this class was a 1 credit class.

One of my favorite professors at cornell! Homework problem sets are extemely hard. If you can do the problem sets by yourself then you are sure to get As in her class. She goes really fast through her lectures but her lectures are very clear and intreasting! You should definitely take this course instead of chem 216.
Horrible, expecially with the group problem sets.  She went way to fast and was hard to understand
No Comments

i actually liked the class and the book. prof hines seemed interested in answering questions in class and in office hours. the class was tough but fair, my experience was that the chem students did better than the chemE's
She was way too fast and unclear.  For a student with minimal chemistry backround, this class was nearly impossible.  Isnt intro chem suppose to help you understand the concepts?

Another really great chem professor! Her lectures, although sorta fast-paced, really got you to understand the material (the graphs were helpful). She really knows her stuff and can teach the intro stuff clearly and intelligently. She's a genius.

Granted, I'm not into chem. However, I enjoyed 207 with Prof. Chirik and expected to at least endure 208 in a reasonable manner. Nope. Lectures were too fast and very unclear, her sense of humor was occasionally offensive, her problem sets bordered on ridiculously difficult (and having groups was a horrible idea), and the exams were similar.

i don't think i ever heard her breathe in lecture. intro chem should be a three day a week lecture. hines wasn't too helpful, and group problem sets are a miserable idea.  i'd say don't take it, but it was a req for everyone, so no choice :(

You can't ask for a better professor than Hines.  She is a great chemist. She does not have the same sense of humor as professor chirik, but she does make funny jokes related to chemistry. She is also very helpful in office hours.

textbook is absolutely useless. never used it once except for looking up constants. the teacher is sometimes confusing. i relied on my AP Chem knowledge rather than her teaching. hw is much more difficult than tests, but prelims and final are easy if you go over the previous exams.
after taking 2007 with chirik...i can't possibly stand hines! she sucks and expects you to read the EXTREMELY unclear textbook in lieu of her unclear lectures  good luck

A true PChemist, she uses math to excess, when simply talking about chemistry would be much more helpful and ultimately more informative.  Geared towards math, incidentally, and if you aren't into memorizing equations and plug-and-chugging, you won't like the class.  I'm not a 5day-weekend cheerleader, just for the record.  Insults aren't becoming.
No Comments

i'm thinking that hines haters are....umm....STATE SCHOOL.  of course you 5-day weekend, cheerleader-turned-****, i-should-always-get-an-"a minus"-at-the-lowest, state school clowns get bitter about a prof. who brings it.  tough problem sets, good lectures, helpful office hours, brutal exams...isn't that what an ivy school is supposed to be about?
she was a great lecturer.  yes, the prob-sets were hard and the labs sucked.  but the exams (which basically determines your grade)was fair.
Very challenging, but I learned a lot.
Awesome problem sets - cool problems and very challenging.  Clear presentation and fair exams.
Decently challenging, exams are fair. Okay class, but not as good as Chirik's 207 (though maybe slightly easier).

Dr. Hines is the best professor, I've ever had at Cornell.  Not only is every lecture lucid, but she also ties many concepts together into one general rule.  Although chemistry involves many concepts, all you have to do is memorize those few rules and you can reason through all her exam questions.

Exams are difficult, problem sets are difficult, lectures occur at the speed of light complete with jokes in bad taste, labs are not helpful. Granted, I didn't like chem to begin with, but somehow I got past it a lot better with Chirik.

Okay, my dislike of chemistry continues with a woman who didn't necessarily teach, but just wrote on the board. She made insulting jokes a lot of the time that just irritated people. I guess she was kinda helpful is you went to her for help, but still. She sucked. The course sucked.
she's no match for Professor Chirik (**very few are) but she is pretty clear in her explanations and quite helpful during office hours
Don't listen to the other people...prof hines is a witch.  Why are her problem sets always about killing people or making bombs or poisonous concoctions... hmmmm.
Don't listen to the Hines lovers. Professor Hines does not make any sense. Her problem sets are evil and her personality...
She's the hottest chemistry professor ever.
her repeated criticisms of new york city got on my nerves.  competent instructor, but i'm not a big fan of her personality or her sense of humor.  group problem sets were somewhat helpful.

Lectures are very clear, and cohesive.  Problem sets are challenging in a good way, they force you to learn the material, and not so hard that you hate it.  The exams are similar to the problem sets and practice exams, if you know your stuff you will get a good grade.  Great and very well run course
She's easily the best chem teacher I've ever had.  Her lectures are excellent and the problem sets are coherent and similar to the exam.
She tries to make chem as easy as possible; shes really clear and you know what to expect on her exams.  She teaches at 175 MPH though, and forces you to take 4 pages of notes per lecture.

This lady is hilarious (with a rather strange sense of humor)!!  She was unbelievably better than McMurry.  She definitely knows her stuff.  You'll definitely get a better grade in her class than you did in McMurry's.
No Comments
A bit boring at times but knows the material and does her best to provide some excitment
Great lecturer; clear one of the best I have ever had for science. Her class is a lot of work and T.A.s are not always that helpful

though not good looking at all, she is very passionate in teaching chemistry and always tries to be clear in what she teaches and very organized. If you do problem sets and read notes, u will be fine on prelims
better than mcmurry

She makes chemistry enjoyable and understandable.  Her lectures are clear and has a strange sense of humor (made fun of handicapped people once).  Much better than McMurry, who simply reads off his textbook while teaching.
she made the material seem understable. however sometimes he goes too much on a detour even just trying to prove a simple point.
amusing and a good lecturer
Clear and knowledgeble.
decent lecturer-- far better than mcmurry.  though the woman does take political incorrectness to whole new levels.  picking on the mentally challenged??  come on...
A bit slow at times, but definitely made it easy.
Excellent prof. Best I've had so far at Cornell. Has an effective method of keeping your attention. Felt like I didn't really need to learn too much from the book after her lectures.
she reminds me of a 6th grade teacher
Great teacher with humor.  Much better than mcmurray
P. Chem is hard, and she made it fairly understandable.
Better than McMurry 'nuff said.
not a fan....
awesome lecturer, definitely the best all year
hines was a godsend after chem 207. i actually enjoyed chem lecture.
awesome lectures, and very clear and precise thought he problem sets were hard, they made me learn

other than Dr. Ganem, the best chem prof. at cornell.  gen. chem SUCKS, but she actually makes the subject bearable.  all of you who are hating on her looks must have nothing better to do w/your time.
No Comments
good lectures
I couldn't stand the group problem set system (forced)...I didn't get it and my partners wouldn't help me, so I did no work and sucked in the class.
CHEM TROLL!
No Comments
No Comments
No Comments
So what that she's not the most attractive person, she made chemistry enjoyable.
I've heard of Girlyman, but I'd never thought of a manlygirl until I saw her!!! This has got to be the MANLIEST woman EVER!!!  EVER!!!
she thinks she's funny.  she's not.
This woman redeemed chemistry for me.
She runs a very interactive class, even with 500+ students. Not too sexy, though
I hated her more than any other professor ever.  She had attitude problems.

Harms is an incredible professor who cares deeply about students' learning. He explains things in a way that anybody can understand how websites are designed and built. His grading criteria are extremely clear — you can get an A+ if you do what's expected. If you use Gen AI and miss the req that's on you. Genuinely the best coding prof at Cornell
reviews.rating_tags: Group projects--Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback

reviews.comment: He manages to make simple things so convoluted with all of his extra steps and procedures. He also is just a very unforgiving and genuinely does not seem to care about his students.

The worst professor and worst class I've taken at Cornell. Don't take his course if u have any other options. Taking his class was the most regrettable choice I ever made. learned nothing from this course, having too much homework and projects, and a bad grade

This class was the most tedious class I have ever taken at Cornell because of the professor. He failed to communicate what he wanted for us on milestones for final projects and graded harshly on exams. His grading rubrics were harsh and lacked consistency.

Exams are annoying, he pulls random things from lecture so you have to basically memorize everything from the slides. No practice materials or study guides either (even for final), practice quizzes only sightly overlap the content on the exams. Tests on things HE thinks is important, not in actual web design or industry. Final was difficult.
Mean professor

Never had such a devious professor in my life. Extremely condescending and frustrating to talk to. Many times the TA confirmed that my work is right, but still got graded as wrong, and there's no room for negotiation. Doesn't accommodate any situation. The class content is easy, but you won't learn anything new that you couldn't have self-learned.

This is an Intro level class, and yet it is harder than most of the classes I've taken at Cornell. Tonnes of homework and projects without clear rubrics so you will have to guess how to avoid any points reduction. Lots of exams as well while this is a project based class. Most of the times TA can't help you at all. Lots of redundant work.

TA's have no clue how to help in office hours because he changes what he wants from the project milestones each semester. TA office hours weren't even worth it bc TA's were guessing the whole time. I asked one if a part of my homework was correct, which they told me was correct, and I proceeded to get no credit on that portion after it was graded

No tests, but also no re-dos on projects so you have to be careful on each project. Group project is not bad at all. Gave extra credit. I went to office hours for help from undergrad grad and Harms and rarely found help. Online resources are allowed as long as cited which is the best option. Skipped lectures so much my advisor reached out...

Prof. Harms can give long assignments, but I did learn a lot from his classes. I think that his classes are worth taking if you are interested in their content. He is also a nice person and does truly care for his students.

Flexible with giving more slip days and canceled lectures in lieu of office hours during class time. Grading could have been more clear but just read the milestone instructions. It is all there. 
Noticed a lot of complaints and confusion during office hours fall from attendance. go to class, it is early but it helps.
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: grading criteria is so confusing.
loads of homework

Incredibily tedious in terms of project requirements. The content itself is not difficult,  but when it comes to project directions, there are so many little things that you have to account for and grading is arbitrary and tough. He doesn't provide you a rubric so you have to guess and imagine all the things that he could dock points off for.
he is mean

Definitely the worst class I've taken at Cornell so far. Filled with boring, busy work. Get ready to spend 3 hours writing actual HTML and 4 hours writing your design journey and hand drawing sketches of each page. Everything about the class including assignment design, is very condescending. Do not take this class unless you have to.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: Loved his class! The assignments are easy if you pay attention in lecture, his demos and online documentation are basically answer keys. The group project was really interesting. He's super accommodating when it comes to the group project, too, and would push back deadlines and change requirements based on student feedback.

This class is rough, BUT, if you really put in the work and grind office hours, you can get by pretty well without attending lectures. This was 100% the most time consuming class I took this semester, but if you just attend labs and OH (can't stress how important this is) you can end up with an A+.
not a fan of him, unfortunately he teaches nearly every single info sci class at cornell

I have had a horrible experience with Kyle Harms. He has been my professor for 2 semesters, for info 1300 (got an A) & info 2300 right now. He is abrasive, condescending, unapproachable, and overall I cannot stress enough how horrific it has been. There are truly not enough words in the English language to express how terrible my experience was.

Had him for 1300 last semester, now taking 2300. There is a lot of project work for both classes but if you go to class, pay attention, and try, its an easy A. Courses are designed so well for beginner programmers. I do not understand all the hate!!
This professor is terrible. I will definitely not take his course again.

Generally reasonable and tries his best for students to understand how to code. Sometimes felt like I was being spoon-fed information. Lots of tedious homework. Energetic, which may be hard to endure for an 8:40am class.

Having the class at 8:40 am every Monday and Wednesday is torture. Not to mention his course style of taking attendance at this point in the day. Assignments were fine and doable but very tedious. The design journeys I get the point but it gets a little too much when done multiple times.

If you take a "Web Design Course" taught by Kyle Harms, just know that you are in for a rough semester. Harms is super un-approachable and rude when you go ask him for help.  He is snarky and pretentious. I do not recommend!

If you take a "Web Design Course" taught by Kyle Harms, you will not be learning "Web Design". Rather, you will be learning "Kyle Harms Web Design". Prof Harms is super un-approachable, and designs his rubrics to grade deflate. If you ask for help, he is snarky and pretentious. He is not accommodating at all; yet he makes mistakes constantly.

Kyle Harms is a fine lecturer, but he is genuinely abrasive and needlessly rude one-on-one. Harms scolded me during his office hours for "wasting [his] time" and that I "was not a priority." No professor of mine has ever come close to his level of unpleasantness. Yes, the class was informative, but the assignments were needlessly tedious.

his classes are project based, so how much effort you put into the projects sort of determines how much time you'll be spending on top of basic website functionality reqs. required participation helps you not fall behind in class, and his teaching style really emphasizes learning objectives and good practices which is pretty valuable imo

Great professor that knows the material he is teaching very well. He wants to make sure we develop good practices, even if the work seems a bit tedious. Listens to student feedback and adjusts accordingly, providing lots of leniency. What he expects is pretty straightforward, you just have to read/watch lectures and project write-up well.
Would recommend, really clear grading criteria

Man was bad, but man has CHANGED.Was struggling in INFO1300 but this time 2300 he has obviously improved so much. He genuinely cared about the students and adjusted the courses based on the feedback. The final project was heavy but he was very understanding. Keep it up KYLE!!

Took his class two years ago and this semester again. I genuinely admire how much he has improved and how willing he is to take in feedback. Never thought I would say this but he truly cares about his students and anyone who doesn't see this should give it time. 10/10 KYLE.

This was one of the classes in that I spent most of my time working on the projects: it was challenging and time-consuming, but after all, I learned a lot and had qualified content for my portfolio. I could see that the professor was trying a lot to figure out how to communicate with the students and organize the class content.
reviews.rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Lots of homework--So many papers

reviews.comment:
He's one of those people you can tell is not only a terrible instructor but also a person you wouldn't want to ever be friends with. Stuck up, condescending, rude, overly critical.

The class concept is simple but he drags things out so painfully, teaches them in weird roundabout ways and just makes everything harder than it needs to be.
man was bad in the past, but MAN IS IMPROVING. This 5 is a recognition for being more accommodating this semester, kyle i know you have been trying and i appreciate it!

If there's a class that significantly decreased my will to keep on going, it'd be INFO 1300. The amount of repetitive work that Harms expects of you, which simply doesn't facilitate learning, no matter what Harms might repeat. An absolute waste of time and a horrendous introduction into the information science major.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--So many papers--Lecture heavy

reviews.comment: professor is quite condescending and doesn't listen to anyone but himself

Both professors seem genuinely enthusiastic for this class, but they talk to students as if they are children, and assign an insane amount of busywork graded only for completion. Mandatory 9 am lectures make this class even worse.

A 3-credit course with a 12-credit workload. A project milestone once a week which takes at least 12 hours to complete and mandatory attendance at 9 am with WATERMARKED attendance sheet. Coding was not hard, but it only consisted 35% of the class. The rest was basically bullshting the design. It was the biggest source of pain for me that semester.

At surface, Kyle Harms seems like a nice approachable guy based off the way he teaches his lectures. However, when you go to ask a question after lectures, he gives you bitter looks and answers. He also does not care for his students. He is not willing to accommodate regardless of your situation; he only wants to do whats convenient for himself.

Grading is literally ridiculous. They are unclear w/ expectations and untimely w/ grading, and take off so many points for the smallest things. And the workload is astronomical as someone mentioned; all my friends know how this class consumed my life this semester. Do not take unless your life depended on it

Truly the worst professor I've ever had, I can't fit everything in this word limit. 

Workload: Unbelievably tedious, repetitive, useless

Harms: SO inflexible, annoying, does not care for students. I asked for an extension bc I had to literally fly home for my grandma's funeral, but he just made me use half my slip days. 

DO NOT take this class

Do not take INFO 1300 unless you are required to. Lectures are very engaging and slow paced, I can actually pay attention at the crack of dawn. However workload is astronomical. Grading is disorganized and "gotchas" that cause students to lose points are NOT made clear. Students are not told how they are graded and don't receive timely feedback

Course material itself is incredibly easy but the professor has made this the most painfully tedious course. I would rather listen to nails on a chalkboard. Professor is also very condescending and teaches as if you were in primary school. If you want to design websites, self-learn the material in several weeks and avoid the mind-numbing busy work
reviews.rating_tags: Participation matters--Lots of homework--So many papers

reviews.comment:
You can learn the semester-long material in the span of 2-3 weeks using the Odin Project. He literally makes you write bullshit and then grades you for not having written good bullshit.

This is a coding class for web design but the actual web design part is like 10% of time invested. The rest is following his stupid curriculum made for 1st graders

This was the hardest class I've ever taken at Cornell. The workload during the first two assignments was somewhat manageable -- 20 hours/week, but the workload during the last assignment was around 40 hours/week. The class feels like it should be at least 6-7 credits, and is insane for 3 credits. 

NEVER TAKE THIS CLASS!!!

The main issue: it's a 3 credit class that should be an 8 - from weekly lab homeworks, prelecture assignments, lecture assignments, weekly project deadlines and weekly lab attendance grades. It's a lot. I would recommend not taking this to learn sql/web dev when you could just learn it on your own. He's extremely strict on grading for no reason.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Participation matters

reviews.comment: too much work, not considerate at all
Honestly just an all-around bad professor. The assignments in the class are extremely boring and require a lot of repetitive work. I would never take another class with him.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: I'm actually surprised by the negative reviews. The writeups could be tedious, but they did help me learn to justify my designs and plan my projects. Professor Harms was a great lecturer and his project instructions were always detailed and clear. Wouldn't recommend if you already know web dev and you're not interested in learning about design.
reviews.rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback--Group projects

reviews.comment: Kyle seems to get more flack than he deserves. Having been in 3 of his classes, it's clear that he wants the best for his students. He puts time/care into planning lectures, answering questions (only getting "mean" when repeatedly asked the same thing), and giving feedback. As long as you're attentive and follow instructions, you'll do just fine.

Would be very tough if you have no prior experience. Harsh grader and takes away lots of points for very small and minor details. I liked the way his lectures were set up but he was very hard to communicate with and every conversation seemed like he felt I was wasting his time. The topic was exciting but he took the fun out of the subject
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

reviews.comment: I'm a non-major, and took this class thinking it would be a fun introduction to web design. I was wrong. The majority of projects are spent on write-ups, and they feel like a waste of time. Professor Harms is extremely passive aggressive and condescending, and hides "gotchas" in projects to make you lose points. Do not take if you don't need to.

This class is unbelievably easy, I think you would have to try to get a grade lower than an A. However, Kyle absolutely ruins this class, he has a terrible condescending attitude, and this class one of the worst at Cornell. I genuinely think he is one of the worst professors at Cornell, and i would NEVER even consider taking one of his classes.

I had Professor Harms for INFO 1300, 2300, and I'll have him next semester for 4340. He's a really nice guy, and he genuinely cares for his students- it was obvious, even over Zoom. There was a lot of tedious work in his web design courses, but all the complaints about that probably come from coders who've never had to justify their designs.
Redundant easy work. I hate the way he talks to students, sounds like dripping in passive aggression.

The majority of my friend group dropped this class, and I should have followed suit. The write-ups for the projects are genuinely unbearable and seem infinite. The course is listed as a web design course, but it feels like an essay-based class. Good luck getting help on Ed, where Prof. Harms response with the vaguest answers on earth. Do not take.

Kyle Harms' class has been my worst experience at Cornell by far. This introductory level course is unreasonable hard, with multiple extremely difficult deadlines each week. The amount of stress that this class causes is unbearable. Because of this class, I have started to attend mental health counseling and therapy sessions almost every day.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Lecture heavy

reviews.comment: There are 4-5 deadlines each week with an insane amount of writing and he's unreasonable and inflexible with grading and deadlines. It's my second year pursuing the InfoSci major but I'm convinced to drop my major because of this man.

Without a doubt, this is by far the worst class and professor I've ever had at Cornell. He treats his students like children (not in a caring way but a patronizing one) and acts like a toddler whenever you try to talk to him. He may appear nice and act as if he cares but he's inflexible and will forsake your wellbeing for his own. Be warned.

This class is abysmal, honestly one of the worst classes I have taken at Cornell. While it may seem like a web dev class at first, the class is much more focused on unnecessary, busy-work writing than actual coding. For the first project in this class, I coded around 1-200 lines, but had to write over 3000 words to describe my "design process."

I was excited to learn about web dev at an esteemed institution such as Cornell. Kyle deserves no esteem, nor is his class a worthwhile use of time, unless you like doing arts and crafts, since all projects incorporate endless design drawing portions, which kill both trees and students' sanity. I also wrote more in this class than I did for my FWS.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Lecture heavy--So many papers

reviews.comment: Only responds to questions with "Yes" on Ed. For example, "Do I use this method or this other method" "Yes".

Honestly, one of the best professors at Cornell. He takes our mental health and well-being very seriously and has been one of the only professors this semester to be accommodating during this pandemic. Also, the lectures, labs, and projects are pretty straightforward and easy to implement if you pay attention.

should be a garbage easy class, and it is, but harms adds all this extra junk with the design journeys and user testing. content is easy but every single task is wrapped around a convoluted menial thing like interviewing users among other garbage that makes this class and its assignments unbearable
ONE MORE THE MOST CHEERING PROFESSORS I HAVE EVER MET! REALLY SAVE ME THROUGH THE CLASSES UNDER COVID TIME!
Fun projects! Kyle does care about students' success and offers a loooot of help if you request for them in class. He tried to make his class a warm community.
One of the best lecturers at Cornell. Lectures were straight forward and I enjoyed the projects as well.

I've taken INFO 1300, 2300, and 4340 with Kyle. He is extremely knowledgable, and is very dedicated to making sure his students learn the material. This dedication informs all of his course policies and teaching methods  but as a consequence he is strict with the rules. Follow his rules to the letter and you will do well and learn a lot.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

reviews.comment: Kyle is an easy professor grading-wise, which is great. Despite this, the class holds your hand way too much. Design journeys are brutal. Easy A though.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Group projects--Clear grading criteria

reviews.comment: Kyle cares profoundly about making sure his students understand the material fully. He is always very energetic considering it was a morning lecture and you have 4 unexcused absences to use throughout the semester to miss lecture. The grading criteria was very clear, thus making it easy to do well in his class.

Honestly, I really enjoy Professor Harms' lectures and try to attend as often as possible, even though it's an early class. He uses a lot of graphics/models when describing certain concepts, which really helps me as a visual learner. It is very clear that he is an expert in his field and he does a really good job breaking down the material.

INFO 2300 is a very easy class, but the thing is that Harms is just really not understanding of any extraneous situation and is really inflexible. I do not recommend this class if you have some kind of coding experience and it's not required because the web design contents covered in INFO 2300 can easily be learned over just one or two days.

Kyle is a very motivated and focused professor, it's clear that he cares a lot about his students learning the skills behind the material that he teaches. However, as a result, there ends up being a large amount of work with strict grading guidelines with minimal leeway. But if you stay on top of everything you will gain practical skills.
reviews.rating_tags: LOTS OF HOMEWORK--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria

reviews.comment: He is a stickler about attendance (takes it every lecture) which sucks for an early morning class, but you can tell he wants us to actually learn the material he teaches. The projects are straightforward and you really get a good grasp of the material. Plus he was super understanding of a personal situation of mine and that meant a lot to me.
reviews.rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

reviews.comment: Professor Kyle Harms did really well for his first semester at Cornell. He really spends time explaining concepts to students and does not skip over steps. He did a lot of live demos at class. He also had the class do engaging activities. Maybe use clickers next semester for attendance.

Easy easy class. Projects can be frustratingly tedious but not difficult. Final exam tested basics and nothing more. Attendance is taken at both lecture and section but you're given a good amount of slip days. Harms is good at explaining things and very willing to help. Class meant for beginners so it can feel a bit slow if you have cs experience.
He has the strictest attendance policy, but you shouldn't miss class. Great at explaining things further. Loves people who ask questions and are generally interested in the topic.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Amazing lectures

reviews.comment: I still have nightmares from her statics and diff eq classes two years later. After graduating Cornell I can confidently say "she" was by far the most unpleasant professor I ever had the displeasure of attempting to learn from. Genuinely mean in the heart, or at least loathes students.

Prof Ritz is absolutely fantastic for statics. She has great intuition and extremely helpful in office hours. Statics is a tough course, but she is really fantastic. Be proactive and she will be super helpful.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Lecture heavy--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Learning outcome system initially seems nice, but makes the last quiz and final exam very stressful. Problems on exams are more difficult than homeworks, especially on second tries of an LO. She's rude when responding to questions in class and on ed discussion. Her lectures aren't the worst but they're not good - I learned everything on my own.
The grading system makes no sense. Really hard to catch up if you fall behind by the first quiz. Good luck!
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: She's pretty polarizing but her notes were very helpful. Also the LO system can be both a blessing and a curse, it was very good if you did well early on and mostly only got 2s and 3s on the quizzes, but falling behind on the quizzes early could set you up to not do very well in the class. The quizzes were fair but some exam questions were not.

The Learning Outcome grading system is not as bad as it seems based on the other reviews. It's easy to know whether you have a solid understanding of each LO from the problem sets. And if you don't, go to office hours. Simple as that. You'll get a good grade. Also go to lectures, I hate the textbook.

In defense of the LO system: This system ensures that if you actually have a strong conceptual understanding of the class, you will be fine. You can revise algebraic/integration mistakes, and can discuss revisions in office hours. You will do well if you consistently put in effort, which is very manageable when spread throughout the semester.
reviews.rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Test heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: worst professor I have had at cornell

The teaching and learning approach often proves ineffective, frequently resulting in numerous issues escalating into a single preliminary or final assessment. The checkpoint mastery grading method appears unnecessary, and no credit is awarded for work already completed.

The whole semester felt redundant losing points over small mistakes in math and overcomplicating simple topics. Too much theory for a class that only tests examples. The mastery system is not good and it needs to be taken away immediately.

First time reviewing a professor here, worst experience ever. The problem with the LO system is that it emphasizes perfection for a course that does require a lot of algebra. The lecture and the textbook aren't great either.
By far, the worst experience with a professor at Cornell.

Grading is completely unreasonable and often unrepresentative of your understanding in the class. Furthermore, she does not foster an environment for asking genuine questions, leaving many students on their own to learn material.

Professor Ritz shows little care for her students, and this applies to her Math 2930 class as well. In the ed discussion, she scolds students for asking questions, saying that they are wasting the staff's time. Both big and small things about how she runs the class make me believe that she does not think from the students' perspectives.
She genuinely grades like a madman. She does not care about your mental health or wellbeing. Always rude and creates the most undoable exams and quizzes.

Like the other reviews are saying, the grading system is.. interesting. Nevertheless, it's not hard to do good in this course ONLY IF you don't fall behind. It's not easy to catch up if you do. Put in the work early in the course, knock out as many learning outcomes as you can on your first try, don't slack off on PDEs, and you'll end up with an A.
don't learn anything in lecture. grading system is very unforgiving towards the end.
GARBAGE TEACHER. LIKE MY RATING IF YOU WANT HER FIRED
Ritz is a mediocre lecturer who is able to semi-clearly explain topics. The grading system makes the class needlessly stressful, and punishes you for making mistakes early.

Ritz is a good lecturer but the grading system penalizes students pretty early on for falling behind. The learning outcomes are checked only on quizzes and prelims and the questions for the learning outcomes tend to be difficult and unlike examples in homework and lecture. The course is more difficult than necessary due to the grading system.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Grading system is not great: not standardized and regrade requests are not taken politely. She does not teach intuition, she just regurgitates the textbook but worse. Cornell has really got to step up its hiring game. Students pay so much to get a good education to be the top students in the country to get this. Way to go Cornell, do better.
Absolutely horrendous at teaching. Very unfortunate.

Love the grading system of this course. It's an easy A if you put in the bare minimum. Go to class, take notes, that's it. Honestly. I didn't even do a single textbook problem set until we started PDEs.
reviews.rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Lecture heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Very clear that she absolutely hates her students. A very discouraging environment
The course itself should not be this hard, but somehow the exam questions are impossible to do that it's impossible to do.

Really clear lectures with helpful lecture notes. Not too much homework. The "outcome-based" grading system (look it up) is innovative and really rewards student effort. This professor got a bad reputation a few years ago, but has improved a ton since then. I would highly recommend taking math with them.

Lectures are decent but she gets passive aggressive when no one participates. Homeworks are very long but exams are easier even though they are still slightly difficult. Decent class but work load was on the heavier side

She is an okay lecturer but I despise the way her classes are graded. For statics, homeworks are tedious to the point where they are not useful for learning the content. Prelims have the opposite problem and are far too easy but you will lose an absurd amount of points for barely doing anything wrong. The checkpoint system for Diffeq is also dumb.

Ritz is genuinely the worst professor I have ever had. Statics is an interesting topic and should be a fairly engaging class, but the grading system is insanely punitive and demotivating. A ridiculous amount of points get taken off for the tiniest mistakes, even the TA's complain about her, and the time commitment is much more than 4 credits worth.

She was genuinely a wonderful professor for statics. Very caring and took effort to learn all of our names in a large lecture. Homework was tedious and difficult but her office hours are incredibly helpful and accessible. Prelims difficulty was very reasonable given what was covered in lecture and homework.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: She's great at explaining things and doesn't move too fast in lectures. The homeworks are really long though, so go to office hours. She's sometimes quite blunt when answering questions but she's helpful.

I'm not sure why she has such a bad rating, maybe she's just better with Statics than with DiffEQ, but I think she's a great teacher, takes the time to learn student's names. Homework is difficult, but it makes Prelims so much easier. I also think the prelim grading is quite generous as I don't deserve a lot of the points I get on them.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Clear grading criteria--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: Ritz is probably a good lecturer, like I'm sure she teaches fine. However, her disgusting attitude overwhelmed any redeeming qualities she has. By far the rudest and most uncaring professor I have ever encountered. Severely lacks empathy.
Never take this course if you see that she's teaching. Honestly, Ritz made me question what Cornell looks for when it hires professors.

I heard lots of things about Dr. Ritz before coming to the class, but to be honest throughout the class she was very generous with grading and such. She's pretty funny and makes an effort to be fair and reasonable. Although the course structure is a little strange, overall it's more helpful than it is hurtful.

Ritz made DiffEq a great experience for me. I looked forward to class and enjoyed learning the content. The content itself can feel disconnected and a bit abstract at times but Ritz made it easy to learn. She made her expectations clear, had good, thorough notes, and was very efficient and effective during office hours.
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Ritz got a bad rep a few years ago, but I think she is great.  She is organized, and will ask for feedback consistently. I was terrified of participating in class, but I still got a lot out of lectures. Her exams are fair, but you definitely need to study. Problem sets are hard, but help is accessible.

If you understand the grading system from the beginning - then it's your average engineering class. I Lecture's were also great. Not a lot of review material available to practice, so join an AEW, do webworks, optional textbook questions, textbook readings, lecture readings.

P-sets are optional, WeBWorks are easy. The Learning Outcome system is still really weird. It's hard to know what your standing is in the course until the very end. Ritz's lectures are solid and, in my opinion, better than the textbook. The final was graded very lightly to fit a normal engineering grade distro, so final grading was not an issue.

Professor Ritz gives really great lectures that you can get a lot out of if you pay attention to them and work on the practice when she gives time to do so. She also genuinely cares a lot about students and our learning, so if you take the time to talk with her, you will get a lot out of that. Exams are tough but definitely fair if you study.

The most frustrating part of this class was the rubrics for assignments and tests — they felt over penalizing for small things. Some HW was too long. Good lectures, the course structure / content was overall enjoyable and well taught. There was good access to support like office hours. Professor Ritz is nice if you talk to her, overall good class.

Whatever happened in past semesters Prof Ritz turned it around. Lectures where amazing. Web work was eh, didn't feel like I got a lot out of it but also not much work. I liked the checkoff system a lot. Felt way less stress over the course of the semester. Focusing on a specific topic without worrying about many others made them easier to master.

This was my first math class where I didn't teach myself from the textbook. Lectures are pretty. It helps to "clear" LOs so that you don't have to worry about them later in the course but sometimes their timing and order fo testing was weird. Slightly frustrating near the end when no practice questions were given on non-homo PDEs.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: This class is not honestly as bad as most people claim -- the course has improved. The lectures were somewhat confusing and were paced oddly, so I often used the published notes. Professor was quite nice and helpful during office hours for revisions. Preparing for later LOs (Heat etc.) was annoying as pset problems did not align with exam problems.
I don't know what the hate for Ritz is. She gives amazing lectures, and really cares about her students. Sure the tests are hard; just study well.

Do not understand the hate towards this professor. The LO grading system is okay if you put in consistent effort across the entire semester and all quizzes and exams. This professor was wonderful in her office hours and her lectures were quite informative even if you don't read the book.
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Clear grading criteria--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Prof. Ritz is able to teach course content with clarity, providing students with plenty of opportunities to ask questions in lecture and review material. She uses interesting demonstrations during lecture (i.e., MATLAB scripts) to describe the physical behavior of differential equations. Overall, I really enjoyed this class with Prof. Ritz.

Ritz isn't the worst, but her grading with Learning Objectives (LO) is. You could be doing good all year, getting 3/3s on LOs on all 4 graded quizzes, and going into the final with 14/21 graded LOs as 3/3s and then bam. If you don't get the answer almost perfectly right you get a 1/3 and your grade is ruined. It's perfection or nothing with grades.

I think her teaching is fine, but most of her responses to questions on Ed Discussion are snarky and unfriendly. She will refuse to give actual answers to genuine questions and respond with things along the lines of "I will not entertain this" or "Why are you asking this?". It just makes me lose respect for her as a person.

For statics ritz has always been very transparent about all of the resources and the criteria to do well in her class. She is more than willing to help you in any way you need to succeed, if only you reach out to her. It's a hard class so make use of the resources.

I will not understand the hate against Ritz, she is mega caring and extremely accessible outside of class. You can tell she cares a lot about her students. For context, her canvas home page has a pep talk for the students. I truly enjoy the grading for this class and it is not as terrible as everyone says: just make sure to not fall behind.

Ritz is the best lecturer I have had at Cornell, for both ENGRD 2020 and MATH 2930. Her classes are definitely tough, but she is always there to support her students and wants to see them all succeed.
This is the worst grading system of any class I've ever had the misfortune of taking. You could do the entire course perfectly up to the final, miss two questions, and get an A-.
What can I say that hasn't already been said? Avoid at all costs.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Atrocious grading rubrics with no thought behind them. Consistently took off 20-40% from total homework grades for tiny errors like not writing dimensions on a Free Body Diagram or not writing "Given: Find: Plan" above the solution to a problem, even when all final answers were correct. Partial credit wasn't given on prelims, which weren't curved.

Says we get multiple attempts-not true. Gave no advice about approach to prelim 1, making prelim 2 a sprint through 9 questions. 2 problems drop a whole letter grade, we got 1 try for them, how this even makes sense when the whole point of this grading system is to have multiple chances. Class material is not bad but just ridiculous grading.
Some people have good karma coming their way. This is not one of those people

Not one to ever use this site, but wanted to publish a review for Professor Ritz.

I truly enjoyed my time in MATH2930 with Hadas Ritz, and I truly believe that this is the most that I have ever learned from a math class. Her lectures are incredibly understandable, and while the grading system may seem odd, she is very fair and it helped me learn.

Disrespectful/snarky on Ed, restricts posting to reduce criticism, doesn't respond to complaints, changes the rules every single day based on mood swings, revokes opportunities, contradicts her syllabus, imposes a draconian grading scheme no one asked for to try to prove research paper she wrote, targeted student who called her out

The new grading system is... interesting. I (and many others) did not like Ritz's course (the lectures move painfully slow), and it covers shockingly little material (no transforms??). The grading scheme doesn't aid with "learn at your own pace" and instead obfuscates what should be a pretty simple class. The material's easy, but grading isn't.

Ritz's grading system where your final grade is determined by mastery is awful. While her lectures were ok, she's got an attitude outside of class and on Ed discussion. Students tried to address some of these concerns and her solution was to set all threads to approval only and tries to silence students in class.

This class was awful; wouldn't recommend it. Uncertain grading scheme caused stress, with assignments delayed throughout the semester. She changed syllabus multiple times, leading to excessive work over holidays. Office hours were chaotic, and criticisms were dismissed as disrespectful. Students unfairly faced consequences due to her shortcomings.
Hadas Ritz is the GOAT when it comes to teaching Differential Equations! She's super chill and makes complex topics feel like a breeze. Love her teaching style!

Ritz is a great lecturer, but the grading scheme she tried to implement this semester was poorly organized and incredibly ineffective. Some things on the syllabus were changed in the last week of class, and there is little/no partial credit.

The class is graded by mastery which is an interesting adjustment to make. However, I enjoyed this grading style. While some of the assignments were very difficult the overall stress around exams was lessened and I believe I performed better on them because of this. Dr.Ritz is also very kind and super willing to help in office hours.
reviews.rating_tags: Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: really easy class, easy grading scheme
grading system makes no sense

I have never learned as much as I did in this class. The homework and material IS very difficult. Ritz asked for feedback multiple times throughout the class and actually applied what she received; it's obvious she cares about students' learning. She is amazing at summarizing the material in approachable ways. Does a good job mixing in examples.

The material itself is not that difficult; she is.
A group of my friends went to find her for help because the exam average was a 70 (and we knew she was not going to give a good curve) -> professor said our year is just dumb. She doesn't understand how COVID exams are easier.

The class was curved to a low B- (she called it a generous curve).
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Professor Ritz tried a new grading scheme that was too good to be true. While it intended to give us lots of opportunities to succeed, it ended up doing the opposite. There was a lack of transparency with the grading scheme, and students were left confused for the majority of the semester, with a huge workload at the end of the semester.

Took ENGRD 2020 in FA 22. Prof. Ritz tries her best to lecture well and convey the necessary material; however the homework sets were pretty long/convoluted and exams usually had a fair share of curveballs. Class was curved to low B- (super annoying) ... be ready for a lot of work for this class with her.

dumb grading system dumb; there were so many important quizzes due during the study period that it was hard to do work for other courses. Missing a single quiz can knock you down a +/- for the overall grade.

The psets are tough but doable for the most part. The content starts out alright but suddenly gets difficult toward the end. There's a harsh curve (median B-) for the overall course grade, and I literally did one std above the mean for prelim 2 and the final and still got a B+. There is "online homework" in addition to the pset, which are annoying.

Grading scheme had a cool concept but basically made all assignments either not worth the time or worth the same as an exam. Every assessment demands perfection or you won't get any credit at all, which makes it way harder to prove understanding than it needs to be. Make sure your TA likes you & be persistent with regrade requests.

The grading was inconsistent/inaccurate. Grading was either full points or no points: no partial credit. There were many times when I got full points on an assignment, and someone with the same answer and work got no points. Or I got no points, put in a regrade request, and then was told my answer was perfectly correct. Disappointing class.

Worst grading system of all time. Does not to partial credit at all. I did not receive credit for several things that I know how to do, because any small algebra mistake means you get no credit for an entire problem. That policy applies to exams, homework, quizes, and everything we do. It is the most awful system I have ever seen
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: very stupid and unclear grading system that requires perfection. First two months we didn't know exactly how grading worked. It caused so much unnecessary stress and anxiety because they didn't communicate with students and assignments were graded very late. There were 6 assignments during study week and some of them were due after the final exam.

Very tough grader. She claimed that the course is graded on a curve and in the end, she said it is not exactly curved which doesn't make sense since the medians for all exams were in the mid 60s. They only slightly adjusted grade boundaries and still, the median grade was low B-. Don't take this course if you don't have to or you will only suffer.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Absolutely confusing grading scheme. Good idea but poor implementation. Students were not informed enough until the end of semester where every assignment piles up and become very stressful.
Her courses traumatize me to the fullest

Avoid at all costs. horrible woman with zero empathy for students. Had her for both ENGRD2020 and MATH2930 in the same semester, would strongly advise against taking either course from her, but especially recommend not taking both from her at the same time unless you're willing to sacrifice both your gpa and many nights of sleep.

Assignments are graded late, no partial credit or mercy. Even going to office hours and getting your work personally checked is not enough, staff might still say it is wrong. Grading scheme felt unfair and does not reflect students mastery of concepts. Is a good lecturer when compared to the rest of the math department.

The grading system seemed appealing at first but it was not executed well at all. Students got drastically different levels of difficulty for the questions. The course was not planned well in that we had 2 checkpoints before prelim 2 and there were 8 due during the study period before finals. Does not address student concerns very well.

I took diff. eq. with Ritz under her new grading system, and unlike the majority of the class thought it was awesome! The grading scheme not only made it extremely easy to do well in the class, it also helped with learning and retaining the material. Ritz is a great lecturer who explains concepts well and addresses any and all question in lecture.

horrible new grading scheme that left students scrambling during finals week. students have no idea how they are doing in the course due to grades not being returned in a timely manner. she assigned ridiculous amounts of work during finals, doubt it'll be graded on time. she's causing stress to students already under lots of stress as engineers

Literally the worst Cornell class I have ever taken. I did well in this class and it still has inspired me to never take a class with professor Ritz ever again. If you see that Ritz is teaching a class that you need to graduate, you're better off taking a 5th year to get there.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: This class was a completely awful experience. I still have no clue what my grade is going to be and she owes me like ten grades. A total failure on her part. I do not know how a mandatory course can be taught this badly.

Professor Ritz fails to organize a half-decent, organized class. Students have little clue how well they are performing in the class until they recieve their final exam. The grading is lazy, as partial credit is simply not awarded, including for computation and algebra-heavy problems. 
Lectures are dull. Online course components are buggy. bad.

Bruuuuuuhhhhhh. Staff cannot keep up with new grading scheme. This has lead to a disorganized mess with feedback coming weeks after its relevant. We also have multiple assignments due 4 days after the last class - which the results of dictate what we need to study for on the final. Absolutey embarassing, and ridiculously stressful on students.

Her new grading scheme is too idealistic and disorganized. Her vision was good (student assessed based on general performance instead of test grades) , but as one of the guinea pig students I can say the disorganization of it all makes it only counterproductive. Turns out we are even more harshly penalized by any mistakes. Run.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Remarkably and unacceptably poor at explaining concepts. Learned through textbook/other resources for the whole semester. Frequently makes errors in lecture, and often changes course structure in detrimental ways. If you can, try not to take this class with Prof. Ritz.
Worst professor I have ever had.

extremely disappointed with this classes policies, especially regarding the hw. Problem sets are excessively long + assigned every week, even on weeks with an exam/break. She also refuses to grade the 10+ page assignments unless they follow her insane formatting to a T. otherwise decent lecturer, just wish staff was even remotely empathetic.

Lectures were fine, not too hard to follow but some rough days on harder concepts. Homework was atrociously hard and took a whole day per assignment, covering more than what was on the prelims or in class. Prelims were terrible for covid semester as they were open book/entirely conceptual, with little actual math done on the exam. Discussions suck.
I was often confused during the semester because the material was difficult, but the lectures were so organized and help was so available that it was not difficult to catch up when I fell behind.
Moves too slowly through material in an uninteresting manner.

She is terrifying. But go to office hours for the homework (it's basically impossible without OH or some other kind of help). She has very high but clear expectations and posts good videos to help with difficult concepts. The exams are hard but straightforward so do the practice problems.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Accessible outside class--Tough grader

reviews.comment: Prof. Ritz was the best engineering professor I had at Cornell. She is VERY knowledgeable, very organized, and was using digital resources effectively even before the pandemic. She doesn't take nonsense from students, but is also not abrasive or hostile to students. She will simply teach you the subject matter clearly and effectively.
Great professor
Just not a good lecturer. Spends lots of time in silence just writing stuff, which makes it very hard to focus. Doesn't help that her handwriting is really hard to read either.

One of my favorites at Cornell! She really cares about teaching, and is great at explaining things, both in lecture at office hours. She was very approachable and made lectures interesting. The class is undoubtedly hard, and you'll have to work for your grade, but there's a high level of support, and expectations are clear. Highly recommend!
Doesn't understand that there's a pandemic going on. Literally so frustrated and office hours were not helpful for this course at all. worst engineering class i've ever taken.

Incredibly off-putting personality, made me hate the subject from the very start of class. On top of this seems to assign work with some sort of vendetta in mind, intentionally confusing students with the mere selection of problems (not to mention the ridiculous format in which homework must be submitted) and then penalizing the confused class.

She doesn't understand that a lot of people do not care about mechanical engineering and how diff eq applies mostly to physics/mechE stuff. We just need the math requirement and will never use that material again
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Graded by few things--Test heavy

reviews.comment: Assigns huge amounts of work even during weeks with exams
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Skip class? You won't pass.--Lecture heavy

reviews.comment: Decent professor. Online homeworks are very difficult. Homeworks are usually manageable. Exams are hard, but fair
reviews.rating_tags: Tough Grader--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--Clear grading criteria

reviews.comment: Class seems to be going well at the start then all of the sudden she throws a bunch of material and assignment at us at a time that we were supposed to be on break. The material that she threw at us was not easy and was not explained at all in lecture, she simply just clicked through the slides without a care in the world for her students.

Great lectures, definitely worth getting up before 8am for. Could actually do the hw on my own. Unfortunately, the exams were written by a different professor so that wasn't too great. Very good at explaining complicated topics in an understandable way and very patient.
reviews.rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--Amazing lectures

reviews.comment: I was wary of Statics, but Ritz was a fantastic professor who really made me interested in the material. The class is hard but not unmanageable if you put in the work - OH are very helpful! Lectures were above average and the exams were fair (although difficult). Homework sometimes seemed like a lot but the problems were worthwhile.

She is a great professor for freshman intro classes, but for junior/senior standing courses she expects more from her students to the point where it's almost too demanding. She gives a lot of difficult work across multiple categories (iClickers, lab quizzes and reports, hws, hit-or-miss exams) which makes it hard to maintain a consistent grade.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough Grader--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

reviews.comment: Lots of good reviews for 1000 level classes, but MAE3260 (Systems)  is a totally different animal. She got criticism last year for the sheer length of problem sets, cut them down for this year, and they are still three times longer than those in any other class. The class material is hard, yes, but her grading policies make it brutal. Good luck.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough Grader--Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

reviews.comment: Good lecturer. Lecture included participation which is unusual and helpful. She would give example problems in class and have you solve them, and give you time to work as she walked around the room to make sure people understood on an individual basis. Was glad to go over things that students didn't understand.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria

reviews.comment: She cares  about her students but often struggles to get a hold of their attention. Not a lot of work with the exception of the final project which takes way too much time for the course's level.

Class super easy except the final design project takes up way too much time fore a 1000 level engineering course. Doesn't really lecture and just goes over problems in class, which is really a better class structure.

Really enjoyed this class. Lectures were usually enjoyable and the material was interesting but not too difficult. Labs can be very frustrating but the final project is a lot of fun. Good overview of mechanical engineering.

She likes doing examples on the board,  always pay attention to it. Doing them yourself helps you really learn the material. Also, don't be scared to ask questions in class, she'll answer and help you learn. Especially if you sit in the front like me. 1910 isn't always easy, going to her lectures will help. So please, don't skip, even if an 8 am!
Prof Ritz is an excellent lecturer - very clear and concise. She moves a bit quickly sometimes, and can seem intimidating, but overall a great professor. The tests for 1910 are very difficult.

Amazing teacher with a genuine concern for her students. She Encourages participation in class, is open to many different views, and facilitates engaging discussions in her classes. Count getting her as a professor as a christmas present.

She is an amazing teacher! Humorous and also absolutely nice! Her lectures are very organized and clear. Although sometimes the materials are difficult, if you go to lectures and study hard, you will be fine! Prelims are fair (although prelims are not written by her). She gives me a good start in engineering math in college!

I would say that Ritz is a great math professor. She is very helpful and open in office hours. However, the class is definitely a challenge... as are many of the first engineering math classes. Tests were written by the other professor, who was definitely a lot worse than Ritz. She also coined the term "Math Happens"!

Ritz is a pretty decent teacher. She's really helpful. She'll answer anything you have trouble with and she loves it when students come to her office hours. She's a really fair teacher. She tests her students on their knowledge, doesn't really seem to try to trick us. I really liked her! Take this class!

Ritz is a great math teacher! She is really clear and helpful when explaining topics and is really funny too! Great teacher, and from what I've heard, the best professor if you are taking 1910. The prelims are fair (I don't think she makes them though..) and the content is about what you would expect from an engineering calc class.
AMAZING!! Funny, clear, helpful!!! Great class, great professor!
A bit strict for a freshman class, but overall very straightforward and helpful.  A good lecturer, plus has very generous "quizzes instead of prelims" policy.

Overall, Ritz is a clear, her class difficulty is fair, and she is a pretty good lecturer.  Instead of giving prelims, she gives ~10 (relatively easy) pop quizzes over the semester (I liked this policy).  She does give a final.  The final project was a lot of fun. My TA (Anoop) was the best TA I have ever had.  Labs were fun.
unclear grading rubric with weekly quizzes
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Participation matters

reviews.comment: She is super nice and smart! I enjoyed her class. Thank you, Prof. Hinrichs!

Very down-to-earth and genuinely cares about her students. Sit-down discussions are very interesting but slide lectures can be a little dull. She is super supportive outside of class for a STEM student like me who is not familiar with college history courses. Would definitely take again.
Inspiring lectures! I like her. She cares about the students!
A brilliant professor who is also considerate! I like engaging in the class.

She doesn't really lecture and I couldn't really get a straight answer out of her whenever I asked a question. That being said, she does genuinely care. In terms of grading, she's not going to give you anything higher or lower than an A-, so as long as you show up to class and put in an average amount of effort it doesn't matter.

Professor Hinrichs is super nice and an amazing person, but her class is super reading heavy (the readings are usually very boring and super long) and the prompts for her papers were super confusing. The essay grading is pretty relaxed, I turned in a few papers that didn't correctly answer the prompt but always got Bs.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Get ready to read--Skip class? You won't pass.

reviews.comment: TJ is really nice and she definitely cares about her students. But, her class has a unique structure that is really difficult to get used to. There is a large amount of reading to do. You discuss the readings and questions in groups and then present to everyone. Very confusing and hard essay questions. She asks you to define everything. Participate
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Participation matters

reviews.comment: TJ is a kind professor who understands the difficulty that comes with dense historical primary sources. Very discussion heavy and participation matters. She's a fair grader but being an active participant can boost your grade. She's is flexible with deadlines and is available to meet.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Participation matters--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

reviews.comment: Course was heavily based on Historiography, not History. Lectures are optional after the 2 in-class quizzes, but section is mandatory. Very essay heavy, with some kind of paper being due weekly. Reading everything assigned would kill you; just read Lewis and Sima Qian and you'll survive. Professor seems to care about students, which is a plus.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--SO MANY PAPERS--GRADED BY FEW THINGS

reviews.comment: Bad, bad lecturer, but seems to care about students. Discussion sections aren't productive. But she's an easy grader, the class average was an A-

She is a great professor. I absolutely enjoyed taking her class. I improved tremendously as a write (better than my FWS). She is really understanding of the competitive culture that exists at Cornell, and will sometimes end class early because she knows we need a break. Readings of about 80-100+ pages a week. Density of the readings fluctuate.
Requires participation in a 200 person lecture and doesn't give you credit even after you participate because she forgot that you participated. Unreal

I had a 94 in the class, 95% on the final, 94% on the midterm, and 100% on all of the assignments. This fm gave me a 20% for my participation grade, which dropped my grade to 89.9%, and I'm pretty sure she did it on purpose. DO NOT take this professor.

Prof Hristakeva's marketing class was more challenging than I thought it would be. Overall, she is a good prof but the exams were graded very harshly. Participation and attendance matter a lot - more than most other courses at Cornell.

I enjoyed the material quite a lot given my curiosity to see how marketing drives business outcomes. However, I feel like we are evaluated less on conceptual understanding but more on the jargons and hard-coded definitions for them, which I feel loses a sense of practicality and applicability for the principles.

Do not recommend this class. Lectures are fine, but grading is extremely rigid and unfair. Exams are 70% of the grade, and you lose points even when answers are conceptually and mathematically correct if wording or methods don't exactly match expectations, which are unclear. Very frustrating.
I really enjoyed this class and the material was very easy to digest and learn. A great marketing class to take.

required attendance/participation, name tags + assigned seating. however, group/individual assignments were extremely manageable. prof gives you SO many opportunities to succeed and get a good grade. class is curved to an A/A-!!!!!!!

Lectures were mandatory, violently boring, with seating charts, name tags, and no laptops allowed. Despite having to do four group projects, three individual homework assignments, and participate in lecture, 70 percent of the grade for this class was based off of the exams, which are primarily memorization focused (closed book).

DO NOT TAKE! Wait one semester! She requires mandatory attendance and makes you wear name tags and assigned seating as if we are in middle school. She treats her students like children it is honestly extremely off putting. Attendance based on participation in class despite it being a 100 person lecture. Also bad accent and so boring during lecture!

Prof Hristakeva, beyond knowing her stuff and teaching it in a digestible way, is a very generous professor. She teaches you exactly what you need to know, nothing more, nothing less, which many appreciate. If you're looking for a super in-depth class, this isn't the one. Her prelims are a hate it or love it kinda thing.

This class is so boring and the material is irrelevant unless you are going into marketing specifically. Exams always had errors and cases were so confusing because the instructions were unclear and TAs also did not understand. This is a class that should've been an easy A, Sylvia's lowkey just making everyone's lives more difficult.
Make sure to participate in class. Focus on understanding the material rather than pure memorization and you will be fine. Homework load is light

The class was okay, with prelims averaging 90%. It had two prelims, a final project instead of an exam, and some group case write-ups. Studying for the prelims was confusing, and lectures were a blur. Professor wasn't around much for help, and TAs just directed you to the slides. Consider taking it with Professor Nasser instead. Heard he's better.

Mrs. Haegar was dedicated to ensuring her students not only learned Excel but also enjoyed the process. This year placed a strong emphasis on AI, and her enthusiasm for what the technology is capable of made it clear how passionate she is about teaching. I recommend introducing yourself to her and the TAs who help make this course such a success.
reviews.rating_tags: Participation matters--Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Originally, I was nervous about this class after reading the reviews. However, she is very kind, caring, and willing to explain any questions you have about the course.
