Perez is very passionate about the course. Overall not bad, but it really had me stressing near the end. As long as you get a somewhat reasonable TA, then it's all good.
Lectures do not relate to assignments at all. Grade is dependent on if you have a good TA or not. One prelim that is based off everything he's taught. Extremely unorganized.

I really liked Professor Perez, he's great and funny. However, his lectures tend to be a bit boring and the class is very homework heavy. There are case studies every week and by the end of the course there are two every week. Grades are dependent on TAs and participation is pretty important. Good experience, but would I take the class again? No.

Pedro is obviously a Dyson rite of passage, and it's fun to meme this class, but it's honestly just annoying. Mandatory lectures consist of nothing of importance, and the case studies are dependent on whether you can figure out how your TA wants you to write. It could have been way worse, but I'm so happy this course is over.
reviews.rating_tags: Group projects--Lots of homework--So many papers

reviews.comment: The professor himself is nice, but basically he isn't involved in the class whatsoever other than giving lectures where he rambles on about nothing really. The biggest problem with this class is the grading system, where your entire grade rests on the TAs. The final project is horrible—TAs said we could try regrade but then brought the grade down.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: This was the worst class I've ever taken. Prof. Perez is actually great, but it's the class itself that's awful. So much work and you don't learn anything. Very anxiety inducing and get prepared to give a high stakes presentation in front of 100 people. Everyone tunes out during the lectures because they don't matter but then we get sm hw.
not good. unfortunately mandatory. professor is quite disrespectful
TA's grade everything so it's not very standardized as a result. Either suck up or fail.

Course is subject to your luck in landing good TAs. TAs grade everything! Case presentation will be traumatizing for some. Case studies will make you crash out weekly. The professor rambles. Final project will make or break your grade. Prelim is just memorization with a few areas of application. Attendance and participation are tracked constantly.

Grade is soley based on your TAs. Lectures are just yap. Prelim isn't too long but just memorization. You get little instruction on how to complete assignments so you're kinda on ur own. Final project boosts your grade from the case studies so just work with your group to get it done and follow the instructions on the website to a tee and ur good.

The professor is nice, however, the lectures are just him rambling and barely has connection to the content. The prelim is purely memorization based. The weekly case studies are really heavy workload with tough grading, and there is no clear criteria. Weekly office hours are basically mandatory, and attendance is really strict for presentation days

Pedro's lectures are very entertaining and dramatic! The class has lots of case studies, and you'll be assigned to a group with two TAs who grade your work. Grading on the case study write-ups is tough. There is 1 prelim & 1 final. Overall, it's a hard but required course, and in hindsight I really enjoyed taking it with my whole freshman class!

Took this as a required course, terrible. Reviewers are accurate: lectures are just him rambling and is not content based at all. For the case studies, you need to go to OH. How the class is for you depends solely on the TA's you're assigned.

Going to office hours outside of normal class time is mandatory. In class lectures have little no to relation to the homework, which is long and tedious. Very vague and unclear instructions, but very harshly graded up to TA's discression. Very unclear explanations of all assignments. Very passionate but rambling lectures.

Super passionate lectures about NOTHING. You will not learn anything in this course. He just rambles on with no direction, and asks the most nonsensical questions during discussions. No idea why this class even exists. Waste of time

The class is ridiculous. Title business management and organization yet the class is anything but well organized. The professor rambles on with zero intention and the classroom is full of students distracted by anything else. The cases are a ridiculous assignment that you have to learn exactly how to write. No clue why this is a mandatory course.

Honestly, Pedro didn't do much in this class other than ramble. He was entertaining, but his lectures are not something you actually have to listen to in order to do well in the class. Just figure out the framework for the case studies and study before the prelim, and stay on top of the final project. You should be absolutely fine/

Pedro is just Pedro. Arbitrary grading by TA's was a common complaint, but I can't say I had that experience. TA's basically run the course, so get to know your TA and go to their office hours each week and participate. Make a good relationship with them because your grade is in their hands. Pedro makes some simple topics quite difficult to grasp.

No set grading rubric, which is a nightmare for weekly case studies. Professor often has oddly specific requirements that are not mentioned anywhere. The only prelim was based on pure memorization and focused on topics that have little to no application outside of the class. Do not recommend, but it is a required course nonetheless so best of luck

Inane class material and case studies, terrible professor, and genuinely one of the worst academic experiences I've ever had. Assignment instructions are purposefully vague, and attendance at OUT-OF-CLASS office hours is mandatory for participation credit. Expect arbitrary grading, rambling lectures, and over-intellectualization of easy topics.

The class is engaging, but the heavy workload and inconsistent TA grading can be a challenge. Utilize Unstuck AI, office hours, and the textbook to stay on top of the material. With effort, you can succeed in this class.

As previously mentioned, this class is based entirely on your luck with TA grading. Prof seemed nice and got us all to think critically. I had 8 assignments that weren't graded and my Final project was graded entirely incorrectly (which the head TA confirmed) and this was not rectified. Prof ignored my emails when I asked about grading

I thought Pedro's lectures were extremely interesting and that he was a fun professor. The class overall is not that difficult once you understand the concepts and most people get As. Definitely meet with your TAs and you should be fine. Also make sure to participate in the class because Pedro does have the final say for grades.

Absolutely loved this professor. Very interesting class and he was amazing. However, it's important to say that the class is mostly led by TA's with the grading. I personally did not like that because my TA was a very harsh grader, but that's just my opinion and the only disadvantage of the class.

Perez is sharp, but the class teaches little of substance. 85% of your grade is in the hands of someone your age who took the course the year before and grading is incredibly arbitrary. The TAs are notoriously unprepared, with the requirements for cases often changing the night they are due. That being said, work hard and you will do well.

Professor is nice; the course layout is egregious. Be prepared to:
1) Attend 2+ office hours with confused TAs since the assignments aren't explained well
2) Get outline of the final project 2 days before it's due (25% of the final grade)
3) Experience different grading rubrics based on the TA
4) Look over 350+ slides for a prelim without direction

At least one case study due a week that got more difficult as the semester progressed. Most of the lectures consisted of him writing something obvious on the board, like "the goal of a business is to make profit" then run around the room and jump up and down. Lectures had 40 slides with entire essays on each slide, which would all be on tests.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--So many papers--Lecture heavy

reviews.comment: Probably going to be a little hard at first but once you get the hang of it, it's pretty okay. Just have to write papers every week, and 2 quizzes that were lecture-based weren't entirely too bad.

I personally loved this class. My biggest advice is go to office hours and get to know your TAs (they do most of the grading)! Final project is a bit long and the prelims are kinda random but just listen in lecture. The case studies are fun if you do them on time. Love Pedro!

I love the guy's energy, but this class is not it. The workload was heavy, and grading between TAs was inconsistent (that is, if your TA graded at all). The previous years had a curve, but this year's head TA decided not to have one. You have to pull off a miracle to get an A if your head TA decides not to curve grades and your TA is a harsh grader

Many complain about Professor Perez because many people in Dyson prefer classes where they can learn math, take an exam, and keep it pushing. AEM 2200 is the opposite of that. If you are a reader and writer, you are literally fine. This class is UNDOUBTEDLY a biggg learning curve, but what you learn helps you for the rest of your Dyson career.

It is way too much work for a class that is this useless. Lectures are unnecessary, but some TAs take attendance. The only good thing about this course is the friendships you make through the communal suffering on Tuesday nights. Besides that, this class completely fulfilled the Dyson stereotype of easy and stupid classes. Total waste of time.
The course appears to be structured but every single aspect of the class is subjective. Vague cases, terrible TAs, pointless lectures that you need to attend for participation.
reviews.rating_tags: Group projects--Lots of homework--So many papers

reviews.comment: unnecessarily difficult class with pointless lectures and annoying case studies. pedro gives very little direction for assignments yet has insane, particular expectations. be prepared to get a ton of points off for things that nobody told you was wrong. he is also pretty rude, he abruptly cut off an entire group's case study for going overtime

Even though there are downsides, Pedro truly cares about his students. His lectures do not affect your grade, but it is beneficial information. If you want to succeed in this class, go to TA Hours and go to class.
Borderline useless class with a ton of busy work. Grading gets easier as semester goes on, but largely evaluated by TAs
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

reviews.comment: The worst academic experience of my life. Notorious for being the dumbest class in Dyson. Insane amount of work. Completely useless lectures that are irrelevant to the assignments. Grading is completely dependent on TAs who often take off points for the most arbitrary reasons. The Professor passionately blabbers on and on about random topics.

Horrible in every way possible. Truly the worst academic experience in my entire life. There is no content in this class. Lectures are a complete waste of time. Case studies are braindead. The course is so bad it is hard to even believe that this is a course offered at Cornell. If I could give a rating of 0 out of 10 I would.

Literally does nothing for this class except lectures that have nothing to do with the course material. Just goes on long tangents about random topics. TAs do all the grading and your grade is highly dependent on their judgement. Tedious weekly essays that take up so much time and don't help at all. Final project is always a mess - good luck :(

Perez is nice, enthusiastic, and knowledgeable. That being said, the class is terrible. Lectures were basically pointless if you did not have to present a case and there were roughly 2 cases due every week. There were also many pointless modules and grades were not given back until months after the assignment was submitted.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--So many papers

reviews.comment: Oh Pedro. Great guy, clearly very smart and caring, but this class is a bit of a mess. You'll never get grades back, and there are a LOT of case studies. Lot of work with little feedback, little knowledge on how to improve your work, really only learned about how to write up a case. Perez really cares about his students though a bit scatterbrained.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Group projects--So many papers

reviews.comment: Disorganized & a lack of communication. Expect to complete around two case studies per week as well as some unrelated modules without any feedback (all case studies I completed September-November were not graded until the end of December). Large final group project. I recommend you befriend your TA because they are the people who grade everything.

If you want a good grade for this class, try to get on your TA's good side instead of the professor. Go to as many office hours (especially your TA's) for the case studies as possible and you are sure to get something in the A range. The class is very easy if you come in with some background knowledge in finance.

Heavy workload. And I just don't understand the optional final policy. You got your expected grade four days prior to the exam, and you have to make an instant decision whether to take the exam or not (and study for it). It just adds too much burden to students. Plus it is extremely unfair for students who already have travel plans.

He should go teaching electrical engineering since he can't stop talking about it. This class made my worst cornell experience so far. Didn't really learn anything from those time-consuming cases and superficial lectures which he tried to make them seem deep.

Terrible, really terrible. Basically you learn nothing. And he TAKES ATTENDANCE, 2 points, 1 for attendance and another for paying attention. Your grade on this class, to a great extent, depends on what kinda TA you are assigned to. So basically, your luck counts a lot if you want to get a good grade.
Great teacher. We had to write a lot, but you get used to it.

If you are not a hard worker, you will not perform well in the course. There are a ton of cases, and they actually are pretty intellectually challenging. Perez himself is incredibly smart, but you really have to attentively listen to him to understand the depth of his lectures. I highly recommend participating as much as you can in this course.

The weekly cases (sometimes 2 or 3 a week) can make the class seem so long. Study the previous lecture for the quizzes. Lecture heavy. The final group presentation project isnt bad, just have to have an understanding of the case. Not a bad class overall, can be on your laptop.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--SO MANY PAPERS--TEST HEAVY

reviews.comment: At the end of the semester, he even refused to tell you your overall score, such as participation grade. Even though he taught nothing in class, he is a very tough grader, and cares nothing about the students' feeling. This is the worst experience that I have ever had in Cornell. Cornell really should find another prof.

Professor Perez radiates passion and vigour. He inspires his students to think analytically about business cases. He entertains as well as teaches, and often jumps vigorously on and off tables in a melodramatic fashion. One learns a lot from his classes. A great professor but one who may not always give his undivided attention to his students.

Prof. Perez enjoys what he lectures and tries to make seemingly-superficial material relevant. The course was somewhat disorganized, but Perez was very accommodating. As long as you do all the work, the class should be fine.
Took this class on a Friday afternoon for about 2 hours. Fantastic discussions regarding ethics and a bit of philosophy with interesting cases. Graded on reflections which are fairly short.

The majority of my work that semester was from his class (case studies and 2 large projects). The quizzes were literally impossible, and reading the chapters they were supposedly based on didn't help. However I  managed to get an A in the class so looking back I'm okay with it. Take advantage of all extra credit and try to participate in class.
You do not really learn that much in lecture, and the quizzes in class can be tough, but overall everyone does well and it is a great first business class at the Dyson School.

One legit question on a quiz he gave us is "Is quality expensive?" (Answer was somehow 'false') Studying for the many tests is almost useless since he will ask the most arbitrary irrelevant questions just to make sure no one gets 100. He's pretty passionate/animated about his subject, but it gets in the way of letting the lectures be clear.
Smart guy but very unclear in what he expects for assignments.

Overall a fine class, but the information presented is utterly irrelevant. My biggest and probably only problem with this class is that he doesn't tell you what he wants and relies too much on the TA's who don't really help at all. But bottom line, as long as you show up you're pretty much guaranteed a B.

Pedro's class is not very straightforward in terms of his expectations or material that is covered. That being said, I did poorly on my prelims, but still ended up with a good grade because I went to class and got all of the extra credit opportunities. Not a great class experience while you're in it but you will be surprised with your grade

Pedro is a a below average professor. His lectures are incoherent, and the class provides you with such minimal information about different areas of business that I left each class without having learned much. His teaching assistants grade unfairly at times and are not the nicest people. I was not impressed or happy with this class or professor.
easy A+ as it is

Get an easier TA! Quizzes are often a surprise, mostly based off of power points, that at times doesn't make complete sense. Participation is needed but pointless as the same idiotic loud students take over the discussions.

The quizzes were, at times, very arbitrary. Perez lacks organization in his lectures at times. The TA grading is subjective and Perez often has mood swings. He is a nice guy though; he will answer questions after class. This class requires a significant time commitment. Make sure to spend a lot of time on the final project.
Horribly confusing tests, useless class, a complete waste of time. Do not take AEM 1200 unless you have to.
Can't stand him

He is extremely passionate about his subject.  His quizzes are fairly difficult, especially if you don't read the textbook.  Also, TA grading can be fairly subjective.  I highly recommend going to TA office hours to talk about cases.  Overall a great and informative class!  Pedro knows a lot and is willing to share if you speak with him.
Terrible tests... super easy with awfully unfair grading.
Great class, workload is very manageable and Perez is a great guy. Highly reccomended!

You never know what you'll get with Perez.  He's polite, intelligent and focused on exposing students to important topics in business.  However, he's also vague, does not explain grade criteria and sometimes does not even appear sure of what he expects.  TA-heavy grading contributes to this.

Mostly, the class is not too hard if you do the work. The material of quizes at times is not what is assigned, but reading the chapter reviews is very helpful. FInal project grades are usually universally very low which brings everyone's grades down.

epitome of wildcard. You never know what you will get with professor Perez. Overall a great guy, but is very hard to understand. You have no idea how you will be graded or what is composed of the grade. Still, its worth having a class with Perez! Awesome Guy!

Took AEM 1200 in the spring (for non AEM majors). Class material was pretty interesting, but, Perez covered everything pretty quickly - for the sake of the exam rather than teaching. Tests were not challenging but the questions were unclear - most were not covered in class and the textbook. We never even received the correct answers before the fina

One of Cornell's best.  He may come off as a stickler only because he expects a manageable amount of respect that unfortunately very little students have.  I suggest taking AEM 1200 strongly, he is an excellent guy.
Pretty boring class, pretty easy though. In the spring he gives practice questions on-line. All the material for the 5 in class tests is on them. Do the on-line questions and you'll do well
I love this man.

Perez is definitely one of the most humorous and knowledgeable people around, but his quizzes' questions can be quite vague and unexpected sometimes. The lectures and books (to a small extent) are helpful, but a good TA helps a lot, especially with cases. Oh, and do participate in class, that'll help any grade a lot at the end of the course...
Pedro is a very interesting and entertaining lecturer. However, his final exam is difficult. He does offer extra credit, which offsets it.

Noticeable Spanish accent (humor value?), too much material to cover in a class. Confusing sometimes, explanations are interesting but not useful for the tests. No homework at least until the final project (~50 hours of group work). Ridiculously confusing & difficult tests even with canceled questions. The extra credit is useful and even necessary.

He is a better professor than people are giving credit for.  Yeah, some of his questions on the tests are ridiculous, but he manages to keep the class' attention.  He actually purposely raised the classes grade by the end, and the average grade was a 86 which is acceptable.

He is probably the worst professor at Cornell. It is ridiculous how the median grade for spring 2010 is hovering around B- range. Even after reading every chapter and taking detailed notes, his tests are biased. Spare yourself the $200+ for textbooks, because the course isn't worth it. And this is coming from an econ major with straight A's.

Pedro knows his stuff but his tests are hit or miss. It is the questions not the material that are challenging. Not helpful with explaining material. Don't even buy the book. Your best move is taking this soph/ jr year after you know the material. He expects you to know the material as well as if you took accting/mkting. Got an A but bad class

The class was a good class. We had two textbooks but the contents overlapped a lot so it was like reading the same stuff twice. The workload was sometimes overwhelming, especially the final project. He gives extra credit and he is very nice. His quizzes are sometime vague but the cases were fun even though some took a lot of time to do.

just want to point out to the poster below me that the average of last spring WAS a B... and this is directly out of the cornell registrar... if you want to be condescending, at least do YOUR research beforehand. I guess it's true then, Perez does seem unlucky in that he keeps on getting such students.
Useless class.  Didnt learn a thing.  As long as you read the simple lists in the textbook you will do fine on the test.  No true knowledge of anything business is actually applied to tests.

Pedro definitely needs help when it comes to teaching (even more so, his TAs which seem to come from the same frat or sorority). However, I often felt bad for him because he tends to get the worst students. Even the ones who post here denote a truly sad inability to do basic research... DIRECTLY FROM THE REGISTRAR'S OFFICE: AEM 1200 MEDIAN: B+.

This is not a good class. He has a tendency to spontaneously freak out. He puts crap on the exams that came from nowhere. He just copies the book onto powerpoints and reads them to the class. I thought I would like this class and was very wrong. It was not an easy A, even though I did all of the extra credit.
don't take it unless you have to. not hard to get an A, but you can definitely spend your time somewhere else. this class is.... NOT worth it. i have learned NOTHING!

NOT AN EASY A! He made up a bunch of crap on all his tests - material was never covered in lecture or in books. No way to study for the class OR learn biz! Means for each exam: 79, 77, & 71. Opt project mean = 85. Even w/ lots of extra credit, the mean grade is B-/B (not curved). FOR AN INTRO AEM CLASS THIS IS RIDICULOUS! coming from a bio major.

One of the worst profs at Cornell. His lecturers are extremely opinionated and his tests are just stupid lists that do not help you learn how to manage a business. His teaching is way too theoretical and doesn't apply to the real world. If you want to learn business management, take a Hotel School course; at least those profs have some experience.

Horrendous teacher. Lectures are boring, and he just reads off his poorly made powerpoints. Has no idea how to design a test. Group project takes a  lot of time, but was the only useful experience I got out of the class.  Perez is an arrogant, arbitrary, thinks he knows it all, short little boy who knows NOTHING. Got an A+ in the class though.

Perez is extremely passionate in lectures, which makes class entertaining and lively.  He makes useful points and follows the book closely in lectures.  The only downside to his classes are tests/quizzes are extremely subjective.  I studied the book and attended all lectures, and reviewed my notes and yet tests were frequently dissimilar

Pedro is not a good professor when it comes to learning in class.  He goes into tangents and does not get through all of his material.  His mannerisms are entertaining but distract you from the material.  Overall it would be an easy class if it wasn't for his subjective tests.  The tests are horribly made and based on his opinions.

This is a relatively easy class, be sure to study the lecture notes and definitions of terms prior to exams- notecards definitely help.  And go to the review sessions, they actually tell you what to study so you don't waste your time reading over everything.  The tests are somewhat arbitrary though, and Pedro is a terrible professor.
hard to understand. can be an okay class, but you have to work for it. exams are somewhat arbitrary
This is one of those classes where you can ship it on a regular basis and still earn a good grade. Or attend it a study for something else.

I agree with the person below me, this class is not too hard. I took this class as an elective, and I can say that it's very easy to get at least an A, and Pedro isn't all too bad. All the complaints you see are probably coming from dumb AEM majors who do zero work, so disregard them.

Everyone complains about Professor Perez, but in reality, the class is 100% memorization.  Getting in the A range is easy if you just memorize all of the random lists on the powerpoint slides (flashcards are a life saver).  Even if you don't like this class and you are not doing well, you will be in love with Professor Perez and his mannerisms

Pedro is not really as bad as everyone makes him out to be.  He tests the material in ways you haven't seen before which can be confusing but there is enough extra credit where, if you put the time in, you can easily get an A. Because the class is so large, if you want help you need to ask for it. But if you ask for it, he's a pretty caring guy.

Take this class if you don't want to learn anything, but want an easy A/A+.People say that his tests are not common sense, because they're not;to ace his tests,you need to study his notes and know his (often supremely illogical) opinions and viewpoints on things.Answer questions on the exams based on what he said in lecture, not based on logic.

Sigh.... what a waste of my time and money. Complete arbitrary lists on test. One of his colleagues said this class was poorly taught and low and behold, it definitely is. Some of his TA's are cool, but some suck, esp J. cancion. She must have chronic TOXIC PMS. There is extra credit & lowest test grade dropped if u do op.project. But again...sigh.

The tests are VERY subjective. The person who said you can have a textbook and still be 70% unsure about the test is SO HONEST. The TAs are really incompetent and do nothing except stand around during lecture. There is extra credit but who wants to run around doing extra credit. The course sucks.

this class is a joke. easy A+ if you are smart enough to go to class and you can write a legible paragraph response to a guest lecturer. 1 view point each week; 1 book report(2-3 pgs); 1 final essay (2-3pgs). if you don't get an A or A+ in the course you shouldn't be at Cornell

take this class if you are good at guessing since the quizzes and tests are extremely random.  the TA's are useless (just like most AEM TA's) and will tell you the wrong answers in the review sessions.  you should probably just buy take note--thats how i got an A after getting C averages on all of the quizzes
Easily the worst professor I have had.  He is an embarrassment to Cornell.

It's a good thing there are many guest lecturers, as Perez does little to capture your attention.  Lectures are uninspired, and Perez abdicates responsibility to the TAs for any problems you may have.  He seems unable to make a decision for himself.  Interesting course topic, bad prof!
Perez is an ok guy, and the material is interesting, but somehow he manages to make it incomprehensible.  The tests are disorganized and arbitrary.

Nice guy! In addition, an embarassment to education, let alone an Ivy League education. I wouldn't let this guy teach fourth grade for fear that the students my develop a habit for drawing pictures on standardized test bubble sheets. If you are are really good at guessing, take this course. If not just watch CNBC for an hour or two.

Pedro's inability to teach is only matched by his personal lack of success in the business field. He will shout very loudly and wave his arms, but then words either do not make sense or are blatantly incorrect. You will do well just memorize the textbook. However, there are bucket loads of extra credits. Tests are irrelevant and largely arbitrary.
Really interesting class! I had a hard time with the accent at first cuz I am not used to it but after a while, I got used to it. He is very lively, passionate and good. I loved this class.
his tests are entirely irrelevant to the material so you cant prepare for it at all.  If it was open book and there was no time limit you would still be unsure abot 75 percent of the questions
Tests are unclear, material is boring.  The only good parts were the guest lecturers and the extra credit opportunities.

Prof Perez is a great guy!  His tests could get into specific details sometimes, but if you got in the 80's on them, you could still get an A in the class based on extra credit and the project.  He's awesome if you ever get to interact with him- definitely make use of the opportunities (like the guest lecture luncheons).  I def recommend the class.

Professor Perez covers the material really well. a whole lot of useful information, and people who kept telling me it was common sense ended up getting a grade in the 70s on the exam. the exams were really are tough. all of the extra credit and doing well on the final earned me an A+. and many people got As with the extra credit.
Given the material the course covers, he does an OK job teaching AEM 220.  But his tests are not very balanced, and your grade is based only on the three tests.  Best to avoid this class if you can.

First off, the person two or three bellow said the average grade was in the 60s - that's not true, his average grades are around 80 on each exam. The exams reflect business situations, which if you haven't prepared for hte exam, you won't understand. He's a great professor, willing to help his students, and he learns people's names! Love the Class!

Hey, what's with all of the Pedro-bashing out there?  This guy is enthusiastic, energetic, and most importantly, he likes working w/ students.  Itook this class b/c nothing else fit into my schedule, but I loved it. You do have to work for your grade, but there are lots of opportunities for extra credit.  Put simply, you get out what you put in.

Pedro was is a good professor but only for this class because you can interact with him on a more one-on-one basis. He doesn't grade anything only the TA's do, so if you have a problem with him it doesn't affect your work. Papers are kind of annoying but the class is required for AEM and he's the only one that teaches it.
I've had four classes with this guy already and more than sick of it. The first two were a mistake and the second two were required. If you can avoid him, I suggest you do so

Don't take this useless course unless you absolutely have to for a prereq.. you will learn nothing useful.  He's a nice guy but he can't teach and his tests are incredibly unfair ( as in the questions make no sense and the answers are all opinion).  The class is an embarrasment to Cornell.

Pedro is a really smart guy but his tests make you hate life. Its not that they're hard... they're really not. Its just that they are so freaking confusing that they'll make you compare fruits to animals (almost literally). I made almost a 100 on one of his tests, and had no idea whether I had done well or not. FYI, the median for that test was 64.

Some harsh words from other students, and I don't know if they are completely warranted.  The tests are tricky (the mid term was at least, not so much the final), but a fair grade is completely attainable if you put forth some effort.  Pedro is a really good guy, and absolutely hilarious.  Take his class, but be prepared to work for your grade.

Perez makes absolutely no sense, & neither do his tests.  This is an example of an actual question: "True or False, is it possible to controll the rate of mental & physical decline that comes with age?"  One would think "True" to be the logical answer, but surprisingly this is not the case.  What are doctors for then? & isn't this an AEM class?

this professor shows absolutely no compassion to his students. his only concern is to make a 2 credit class feel like a 4 credit one. ridiculous statements such as the book is wrong are common occurences in this embarassment to cornell university.
Very unclear teacher. All questions have multiple CORRECT answers but he doesn't know that!

Prof Perez is a great guy and he manages to pull off great dynamics in such large lectures.  He writes tricky exams.  The TAs are really helpful so utilize them.  Overall a great introductory class, but it's not a guaranteed A, and Prof. seems to take a lot of crap because it isn't.  It's not very fair of people to complain bcse it isnt an easy A.

Class is a waste of time, everything is common sense and incredibly boring. seems like the easiest class until you get to the prelim, which was the most poorly written test I have ever taken, dumb questions on minute concepts, and answers that disagreed with the textbook, made class average terrible.

Perez is the worst professor I've had at Cornell.He doesn't know what he's doing AT ALL - its a waste of life to actually show up to class.The professor favors people who really suck upto him a lot,and never knows what he is talking about.He tries to make it hard by asking sneaky questions & making a big deal of stupid stuff, but u learn nothing.
Overall class is good for those who don't have much background in business.  Tests weren't bad but felt somewhat unreasonably tricky on some parts.

This is the easiest class I have yet to take, and professor perez is phenomenal if you listen to what he has to say. You can learn a lot from him if you are genuinely there to learn and not for the grade, he seems to know everything about anything you ask about-take time to get to know him if you can
worst teacher ever. period.

Not a very good professor at all, his choice of undergrad TAs were very bad. He never responds to emails in a timely fashion, the secretary for the class is not friendly, and the way they graded our huge projects is insulting. Overall, one of the worse professors I have had at CU. Very nice man but as a Cornell prof, I hope he doesn't get tenure.

the most disorganized class i have taken . i took the class for fun, to learn business. i havent learnt a thing. he squeezes in too much information in one lecture. he is a relaly nice guy , and i hate to say anything negative, but he should have GRAD TA's in his class. undergrads dont know how to run the class. and HIS OPINION answers are only rit

I turned in a 30 page project on one day, and they graded and returned it on the next.  There is no way they read every student's project and returned them in one day.  (the grades werent stellar, either). enough said.
class is incredibly disorganized.  tests are tricky and confusing.  lectures are boring.

Pedro's lectures are just taken straight from the textbook, and would be pointless if he didnt have the mandatory attendance with the clickers. his prelims arent that bad, but you have to memorize a lot of useless info.
No Comments

He relies on his TAs to do all the work, who are definitely not there to help students. Nice guy but I haven't learned anything useful. His tests require you to memorize that useless info.. you'll end up with an OK grade with extra credit.
No Comments
great guy/posts slides online

Do not take this class. The exams are extremely tricky. They totally screwed up my grade and added everything up wrong and now when I am trying to contact them to change it they are just ignoring me. Also, you will learn absolutely nothing important.

Let's play a little game... the name of the game is easy. Go to class (eh-viewpoints), do the extra credit, and it wont even matter how you do on the test. If you do not get an A in this class you shouldn't be at Cornell, period.
fun and entertaining professor. points come across clearly..easy A with several extra credit opportunities such as viewpoints and experiment participation.
fun and entertaining professor. points come across clearly..easy A with several extra credit opportunities such as viewpoints and experiment participation.
It's an easy A.  Pedro Perez (P.P.) is hilarious.  The class is pretty much common sense, but it's fun to attend just to see PP do stunts like jumping on tables and screaming at people.
No Comments

this class is deceptively easy, the midterms is killer and there aren't tons of opportunities to make up los points. the class material is a waste of time, either you get it or you don't. do the reading to study for the ridiculously unrelated test.
the material is easy

This class is a waste of time. Easy A, but a terrible professor and terrible curriculum. Every lecture you go to makes you dumber and dumber. Do not take it unless you have to - and if you do take it, don't go to lecture as much as possible.
This class is very easy.  The material is very easy to learn, and Pedro sounds like Desi Arnaz.  You can't get much better than that.

This class should be renamed Common Sense 220.  Frankly, I found the material pretty boring, but Pedro is entertaining and a nice guy.  The one thing that bothered me is he never responded to my emails, so I dock him on helpfulness.
pedro! pedro!  he's great, he remembers people's names, and helps you get internships, and he's just an awesome guy.  highly recommended pedro!
Pedro, you rock!

Very interesting class.  Not much work in his class, and lots of extra credits.  If you want to have your own business in the future, these two classes will help you to shape yourself and gain knowledge of how to be a entrepreneur.
Great class. Very enthusiastic professor.  Teaches well, very real world material.  Gives various opportunties to make you do well.  Take this class.

Perez is like the Venezualan father a WASP like me never had!  He's the nicest guy, the most dynamic lecturer.  He's one of the few profs that teaches a class of 400 kids and gets more than 3/4 to come to every lecture.  TAKE HIS CLASS!

I thought this class was extremely pointless.  But, he allowed us to improve our grades and had a lot of extra credit opportunities.  I personally did not enjoy his teaching style, and he has an accent, which makes it a little harder to understand him.
Great lecturer, very interesting and clear. Gets the point across really well. Class recommended as an enjoyable introduction to business. Lots of room for grade improvement.
Very easy course, entertaining leactures, and an over all great professor. There's no stress here.
Interesting professor, entertaining.  Lots of extra credit!!!!!!!!!!
No Comments
Nothing's better than the AEM department for an easy A!
What a freakin pointless class.  Cornell is so retarded for making us take this class!

Did not learn a single thing. Never taught in class, not once. Misinformed me about when the last day of class would be and made me miss a crucial assignment. Then when asked about it, her response was that she believed it didn't matter since I "wouldn't have done well on it anyway". None of my peers nor I took a single thing away from this class.
It was very hard to learn from her because of her language barrier, bad explanations in videos, and structure of class.

For how ever many supposed "degrees" she has, she actually might be one of the least competent profs I've ever met. She has zero common sense. She makes tests intentionally hard, tricky, & unfair. Just know that email-her and in-person-her are two completely separate personalities. Email version chooses violence. IRL version is sweet as pie.

Conniving. Her flipped classroom method is very ineffective. Class starts really easy and gets unimaginably complex very fast. She seems nice but don't be fooled, she's not. She doesn't know how to teach because she herself just finished school. Sections get treated differently, you'll see why. Just try to keep up and work with others

BS from Tsinghua; PhD from UC Berkeley. She's “nice,” but the flipped classroom feels like buz 💻, which is expected. No rubric, so assignments are unclear and feedback is vague (“minus 1 point for blah”). Individual and class assignments are tough; the curve spans all sections—think twice before sharing what's on the exam 🫵🫵🫵 no partial credit

Did not like the class structure. Professor Meng is very nice and accommodating, but you learn nothing from the class, even if you get a great grade. Sometimes the grading criteria are also a bit confusing, but overall not hard to get a good grade.

Flipped-classroom. You copy videos for homework, then do a harder version of the content in class. No explanation of the material. In-class TAs do not grade and cannot provide clear feedback or help, and the grading criteria is unclear. Students submit identical work and receive different scores. Assignments due @ the end of class. A waste of time.
You genuinely learn nothing
she's a nice person, but i genuinely learned nothing from her classes.

The class is flipped and you have to come to class with the model prepared. If you watch the video and/or read the notes before class you will do well in the class. The quizzes/prelims are open internet and are similar to the models used for the regular assignments. If you come prepared and do the bonus you will do very well.
reviews.rating_tags: EXTRA CREDIT--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Terrible teacher. Flipped class. All you need to do is copy what she does in the videos and you don't have to learn anything about statistics. She doesn't make her grading guidelines clear either.

Ernesto was the most laid back professor I've ever had and this was probably the most chill class I've ever taken. Also the second half of class you watch movies once a week and discuss the other day, final project is also a 5 min video on any topic you want. Goated class.
reviews.rating_tags: Caring--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Super laid-back. Classes with him are really relaxed. He points out great writing advice and tips I never knew about. He's just not too into fantasy writing and head's up: not good with remembering names. I've taken two classes with him.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Gives good feedback

reviews.comment: Ernesto was my professor for True Stories (FWS). He is a great and will really work with students. He assigns few papers and gives good feedback. The class is about radical political movements in the US, and we sometimes watched movies and did light readings (less than 5 pgs). Would 10/10 recommend this class to anyone!!
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: The fact that this is my first time giving a review here is testament to Ernesto's teaching ability. He's sharp, open-minded, funny, experimental, inspirational, and doesn't BS. He won't sugar-coat, but he will make you a better, more aware writer. And if he pulls you aside to encourage you to send your work out... you know you've made it :)
If you put in the work, you will do very well.
Friendly, but provides no useful feedback and no help improving work. Doesn't pay much attention. Easy grader but you learn nothing.

Didn't think creative writing was going to be this interesting.  Ernesto, and he never wants to be called professor, would play a film clip and then have us read a short story and lead us to see the same narrative techniques being used in fiction. Amazing!!!  He is very cute but he wears the same black shirt a lot.  Easy grader.
Interesting guy, extremely laid-back and passionate about the material he teaches. Dynamic, creative professor. Very easy grader.

Professor Qian worked with me on my doctoral thesis and is a lifesaver in every definition of the word. She was the main advisor for our research team and saved us so much time and energy on our projects. She helped us get our findings published, present at conferences, and advocated for us at every step of the way. Can't say enough about her.
reviews.rating_tags: Hilarious--Beware of pop quizzes--So many papers

reviews.comment: Probably the best professor at Cornell. She's a great mentor, accessible, and willing to work with you on every level. Taking her class redefined my entire life and career.
So great to have a professor that actually cares about their students. Her lectures changed my life.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Online Savvy

reviews.comment:
Dr. Qian is one of the most intellectually stimulating professors I've worked with at Cornell. She pushes us to question assumptions and find design solutions that are both elegant and ethical. She also genuinely cares about her students' growth. 

She gives tough feedback, but it's always constructive and deeply insightful.

Dr. Qian was definitely challenging, but she's incredibly supportive and always willing to talk through ideas or give thoughtful feedback.

By the end of the semester, I felt like I had leveled up as a designer and as a thinker. If you're ready to put in the work, she will absolutely meet you there and help you get the most out of the process.
She's the best professor I've ever had.

Loney is honest & funny, but MATH-1100's flipped format relies on a TERRIBLE textbook that sets students up for failure IMO. 

The real problem is grading--which is shared with Prof. Kaminski whos unbelievably harsh, inconsistent, & nitpicky. So be aware, if you take Prof. Loney in the summer, you're stuck with her grading too.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Gives good feedback--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: Quincy has to be the worst professor I've ever had. He would often get confused by his own questions, struggle to explain basic concepts, and nitpick every HW calculation to his exact standards. Be prepared to teach yourself. He is a horrible professor, and a rude person who tries to make you feel dumb instead of helping you during class.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: Professor Loney (wishes to be called Quincy) was an extremely animated professor. His course was super difficult for me, but most students seem to do just fine. A majority of the course was all about the textbook so the textbook is a MUST! He's super excusable outside of class and only wants to help the students succeed.

Very personally invested in teaching and improving every student's Chinese skills, though that sometimes means being harsh with her students and assigning large amounts of memorization-heavy homework. She takes attendance and diligent completion of homework seriously. Her grading practices are predictable; you directly get out what you put in.
reviews.rating_tags: Participation matters--Clear grading criteria--Lots of homework

reviews.comment: Pretty strict but wants you to improve. Skilled at teaching the material and getting conversations going. In class exercises are repetitive but improved pronunciation a ton. Sizable daily HW + daily writing quizzes. When you make a mistake, she is strict with you, making you repeat until you get it correct, which can be stressful (Whiplash vibes).
reviews.rating_tags: Participation matters--Lots of homework--Beware of pop quizzes

reviews.comment: It's a ton of work, but my Chinese did improve a lot, both over CHIN 2201 and 2202. I wasn't on her bad side, but if she doesn't like you, it gets a lot harder for you to finish out the class.
My Chinese improved a lot after taking Intermediate with her. She really wants everyone to practice as much as possible, which was encouraging.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Clear grading criteria

reviews.comment: She's very helpful and reasonable, but it is a tough class with many pop quizzes. I had to work hard, but I am now able to spend hours with my Chinese friends without speaking any English, which ultimately is the only true measure of a language teacher and course.

Took both Chin 2201 and 2202 with her, she can be really funny sometimes and make the class interesting with some fun dialogue. However, she sometimes carries the crazy high maintenance mother persona which scares me. Besides all that, shes a really great Chinese professor who will teach Chinese in a very painless way and make you laugh.

I took IMPAC over the summer with Teng Laoshi and liked her. She has her moments of snappiness and strictness but usually she's really nice and patient. She also teaches you a lot of useful vocabulary that isn't in the textbook, which is somewhat obscure. Her tests are manageable and her expectations are reasonable. Teng helps move students along.

Compared to He Laoshi in first year, Teng Laoshi gives students a much more time-consuming workload. And she expects all students to have a fairly high ability to understand conversation. Her teaching style may not be the most effective nor lucid for everyone, but students at least get a solid background in vocabulary and grammar, if nothing else.

Though I hear she's a lot harder on her 200 level class, I took 4411 with Teng Laoshi and loved every moment of it. Relative to the other chinese faculty, she's easily the most sane and reasonable, plus probably the most intelligent and driven. You'll learn Chinese with her and not hate your life doing it.
Nice, helpful, knowledgable and interested professor.  Some of her in-class review games are a little tacky, but they get the job done.

Don't know why Quan has so many haters. You can literality fup the Prelim (miss almost half of his 25 MCQ exam) and still get an A. He is THE BEST professor in hotel school. Years of experience in  Real Estate. 

YOU need to forget about what your $10000 career coach tells you. Just go to class and learn!!!

One of the worst classes I've taken at Cornell, 100% wouldn't recommend. Posts no grades, exam averages are god awful, and curve isn't published until grades are finalized on transcript. He reads off of word documents during lectures and makes them unbearable. Called guest lecturer by the wrong name and fell asleep during another guest. No good.
reviews.rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: tests are extremely tricky... lectures and slides do not prepare you well enough for them. all MCQs, no partial credit. answer choices include a lot of none of the above, not enough information, or all of the above. easy for him to grade but hard for us to take as test-takers.

Professor Quan tricks you the first day. He makes fun of his grades on rate my professor which makes you think that they are fake however they are real and he is one of the worst professors at Cornell. Do everything you can to avoid him.
reviews.rating_tags: Lecture heavy--Test heavy--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: His grading can be a bit obscure with one M/C question being worth 15% of an exam and his case studies are a headache but overall good professor. Not impossible getting a good grade. He'll give you an A if you do better than everyone else as its subjective at the end of the semester. (grad students tend to be bricks and bring grade average down)

Quan sees himself as infallible, which is ironic because he is the worst professor I have had at Cornell. He's defensive and will snark students. During lecture, he mindlessly reads off Word documents. He does not prepare you for the case studies at all - e.g. he just assumes you know how to make a 2-tier JV equity waterfall on excel lol. Good luck

Professor Quan sucks the life out of real estate. Your grade is mostly consistent of 2 exams, both only 20 multiple choice questions designed to mess with your head (lots of "not enough information"). Lectures are dry as he only reads off the same pdfs he uses every year. If you can avoid him, please do!!!

The worst class Ive taken at Cornell by FAR. The classes quite literally are just him reading the lectures and hes a horrible lecturer. Tests are nearly impossible, and he doesnt respond to emails with questions either. Should really consider retirement ASAP since it seems like while alumni from years back respect him, current students do not.

Literally just reads off of the notes. Class is miserable to try to sit through. Sasses people who participate. Your grade depends on 2 home-works and 2 exams that are only 20 multiple choice questions. Did not learn anything.
Makes no sense. Can't understand professor Quan. He rambles during class.
difficult class but one of the most rewarding! many drop out after the first prelim but should def stay around. GPA isn't everything..this class is great learning!!
GENIUS
Excellent professsor
If you don't take a class with Quan you are missing out on one of best professors not only at Cornell, but in the Ivy League

Prof. Quan is amazing.  His real estate and finance knowledge are unsurpassed, and he's got some great contacts when it comes to job hunting.  His courses aren't easy, but you'll get more out of them than 99% of other Cornell courses.

Obviously biochem is going to be hard, no matter who teaches it. I thought Prof. Qian did a good job of presenting the material in as accessible of a way as possible. He was also really kind and somehow kind of funny. I appreciated him even more compared to next professor who switched in halfway through the semester.

He is a demanding professor and covers a lot of very important content. That being said he does move fast and it is hard to keep up at certain times in the semester. I spent about 10-15 hours a week fully digesting the material and it was a course I will remember because it reminded me why I love biology. Would take it again in an instant.
reviews.rating_tags: Respected--Accessible outside class--Skip class? You won't pass.

reviews.comment: Honestly, I felt like he went pretty fast through the slides during lectures, and he wasn't very helpful during office hours. Make sure you go to class because he will test you on things he says, but are not explicitly in the slides. He did throw in a few jokes though. Not the most understanding prof. The TAs will be your best friend.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Skip class? You won't pass.--Lecture heavy

reviews.comment: He throws a lot of information at you at once so be ready to record his lectures. He also is hard to understand sometimes. The test questions are based on minor details so when you study make sure to throughly look through your notes.

Professor Qian is one of the worst professors I've had. Hard to understand lectures. Prelims are ridiculous true/false questions that don't test actual understanding of material. Very rude! Makes fun of questions in lecture. Doesn't accommodate students needs. But! It's easier than the other biochem classes and is more pre-med/vet focused.
Good professor in general! But the material covered is more like cell biology and I am a bit disappointed by that... Would love to see more chemistry.

Professor Qian definitely challenges you. If you want to do above average in his class you need to put in the work for it, but he's extremely helpful if you utilize his office hours. He wants to see you succeed and definitely believes in you.

Took NS 3200 in Fall 14.  Did not like this class and would not recommend. Professor Qian taught 34 of the class and he was not very clear due to his accent.  Also, by cramming it all into one semester, you do not go very much in depth. Fell more like a Cell bio than biochemistry. Easier than 3310 but I would recommend  3310-3320 to learn the most

Avoid if possible. He is not easy to understand and his lectures are so lackluster.  Even though I found the material interesting, I only understood the slides by studying them on my own... basically, Khan Academy was my best friend taking this class.  He is terrible at answering questions.  His exams are manageable, but nonetheless difficult.
Genuinely the kindest professor I've ever had, you won't regret taking a class with him.
Such a nice guy!!

RVR is one of my favorite professors I have had at Cornell. Knowing that you're being taught OS by someone who worked at Bell Labs with the creators of Unix and C is very compelling and inspirational. He is hilarious and his office hours are immensely helpful, more so than TAs. He is also very active on Ed. His demos in class are also insightful.
Man is a legend of his field. Super smart and caring!

I loved having RVR as my professor. He can come across as disorganized at times, but it wasn't a big deal for me. I found that he was a hilarious professor that obviously knew the material he was teaching so well yet was so down to earth and super approachable. I thought all the homeworks and projects weren't difficult and the prelims were fair.
Prof. RVR's lectures are quite good, even if they move slowly.
good professor but class is often disorganized. grading and exams aren't representative of the content covered in lectures.

RVR's weekly assignments were sized appropriately and reinforced the lecture materials, reasonable workload. He was helpful after lectures in answering questions, and walked through difficult assignment questions. I wasn't a fan of his exams and this was my downfall - it required a deeper understanding of concepts than what is provided in lecture.
He's one of the most genuinely caring and personable CS professors I've had at Cornell  I feel lucky to have him as a professor tbh
he is a really caring person, and I found his lectures easy to follow
the whole course is a mess and won't teach you much stuff. The professors and TAs are unhelpful. You'd rather self-study instead of taking this class
Although he is kind, its clear he did not spend much time preparing for lecture. This resulted in a very disorganized course.

Excellent lecture notes (probably the best lecture organizer), accessible outside of classroom. He writes very hard exam problems but curve is very generous (Median A- in 2240). Course itself is worth taking.
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Great math class if you want to truly understand linear algebra and multivariable calculus. Expect a LOT of work, but no busywork. For comparison, it is significantly harder than CS 2112.
Lectures are extremely informative, and explain the content even better than the textbook (and the textbook is very good)
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment:
Pros: Good lecture notes, well-paced, good exam curves, no attendance required at all
Cons: Sometimes cannot understand student's questions, TONS of homework (10+ hrs per week)
I liked his lectures because he always goes through proofs and the "why" so conceptualizing is easier. MATH 1920 as a course was horribly hard though, but if you practice a ton you'll survive.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Respected

reviews.comment: Prof. Sjamaar always had well-organized lecture notes and I found in-class examples useful for completing homework. Some find his lectures boring, but he always explained material thoroughly. He may be a bit dry in office hours but he is helpful and understanding. Class is hard, but doing practice prelims will get you through.
Took his class for Math1920. Class was very test-heavy. Stopped going to his lectures after a couple of weeks. His lectures were boring and hard to understand.

honestly don't go to the lectures its not worth it. you will get so much more out of just reading the textbook and taking notes. the final for this class was the hardest test I've ever taken in my life (as was agreed upon by everyone I talked to after as well) but the curve made up for it I guess? Idk just try to persevere guys.

Nice and can be funny in lecture. Lectures weren't necessary to succeed, but I went to all of them (8am, very rough). A bunch of topics def could've been explained better. Def recommend using the textbook, do all practice prelims given, & learn how to do all those problems for the prelims. Didn't appreciate that the prelims were curved down.

Lectures are hard to understand unless you read the textbook beforehand. Professor often doesn't know how to explain the material in a helpful/applicable way—there seemed to be a disconnect between lectures and HW questions IMO. however very chill and nice person, recommend going to office hours and you'll be fine
The lectures are hard to understand without having read the textbook beforehand. But overall multi is a hard class and Sjamaar does a decent job at teaching.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lecture heavy--Test heavy

reviews.comment: Hard to ask questions. Scary and Industrial Class.
Great lectures and absolute icon, great analogies. Textbook problems are similar to the quizzes, but exams are different. Practice prelims are helpful.

Very cool and calm dude. Lectures organized very clearly, amazing handwriting, and explanations are very helpful. Very patient. Just go to lectures, go to office hours, and ask questions, then you'll be fine.

Seems like a nice professor who wants you to succeed. Lectures were pretty enjoyable and even though the content was difficult at times he was pretty good at explaining things. Exams were pretty hard but not unfair, and the curve was pretty generous. Overall he was a good prof to take this course with

This class was pretty tough for me personally. I found lectures difficult and unclear and the textbook was not great at explaining certain topics. Quizzes during discussions were alright since they closely followed the homework, however, tests extremely difficult and unfair. Also ed-discussion responses were a bit mean sometimes.

Gives lecture note templates, neat and structured lectures, sometimes monotone but always makes sure he goes over every topic. Gave us one letter page, front-back, cheat sheet for exams. Just read the textbook, and attend lectures, Sjamaar is a solid professor.

Tough to sum up my 1920 experience with Sjamaar. For some reason the tests were exactly the same as 2019, and he had to change the final grading scheme to make it a little fair for those who had studied.

As others have said, the lectures are well organised and concepts are well explained. However if speaking with him after lecture (or even answering questions in lecture), his manner often makes you feel stupid. His responses on Ed are very abrupt. Overall would take again, just don't except any warmth.

Professor Sjamaar is a great professor; his analogies are funny and useful, and he has a pleasant Dutch accent.

His favorite word is orthogonal, and his office hours are always popular. I would recommend him, he's a memorable lecturer for sure!

I suppose 1920 can be difficult, but he made it enjoyable; it's always help to Read as well :)

He is very well organized and good at explaining/naturally developing concepts. His presentation of the content is at times much superior to the one given in Hubbard & Hubbard (the textbook). I'd recommend going to his office hours, he often has really interesting things to say about any question you could ask and is enthusiastic about helping out!
Very good lecture organization. Most lectures are not very compelling however. He can be intimidating outside of class. Hard to connect with.
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Personally, his lectures were ok. They were extremely middle of the road. However, his review lectures were exceedingly helpful towards getting a good score on each prelim. Overall, I think my grade mainly reflected my own reading of the textbook rather than the quality of his lectures and his teaching style.

Good class, but you have to study pretty well to do well (I slacked off before the first prelim and highly regret it haha). Lots of available resources provided by the prof, just have to make use of them. Additionally, really reached out to help students especially during this weird covid era.

Some might find his lectures a bit dry, but he is such a wonderful professor. Explains things super clear and is always happy to answer all sorts of questions from students. I attended both his and another professor's class for math 1920, and Sjamaar definitely has it more organized. 10/10 recommend him.

Came back to rate him after 3 years. Best maths prof I ever had - halfway into the semester I literally gave up my original lecture session and skipped lunch to attend his. He lectures and writes on the board clearly. Also gives interesting examples every once in a while. Students are encouraged to ask and answer questions during lecture.

Lectures were useless. Just went over theorems and a couple examples. Did not help from the problem solving aspect, which I felt was most needed. Prelims were very difficult, especially the final. I wish detailed answers were given for the homework. And forget about being able to sit up close if you don't get there 20 minutes before class!
reviews.rating_tags: Tough Grader--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--GRADED BY FEW THINGS

reviews.comment: hardest class ive taken. There is hardly any partial credit on prelims and NO continuation points. When i asked why there are no continuation points, the sjamaar simply replied that it didnt exist! this class is more about being careful (when hardly any time is given for exams) than understanding concepts i guess! glad its over.

Very good choice of a professor. Lectures can be a bit difficult if you don't sit near the front as he has a bit of an accent and a mumble - but he will throw in a little joke every once in a while. He is also willing to stop and answer questions during the lecture. Although tests were hard, it wasn't unreasonable given what was covered in lecture.

Pretty solid lecturer; his teaching is a bit dry, but he throws in the occasional joke to lighten the mood. He definitely knows his stuff, and doesn't mind taking the time to answer questions or explain in further detail. The 1920 exams are absolutely wicked, but he does a decent job preparing for them.
reviews.rating_tags: Respected--Get ready to read--Skip class? You won't pass.

reviews.comment: His lectures are pretty useful overall, having a good mix between theory and examples. Math 1920 is a hard class overall, but he does a good job of making sure we have a grasp on the material. He really seems to look out for his students, and he's always willing to stop lectures to answer questions.

He was a solid lecturer. His lectures started with the theory and he then worked through good examples. I liked to look over the chapter summaries from the textbook before lecture (those chapter summaries were amazing). And quick note: he typically finishes class 1-5 mins late.

Very nice person. He is very passionate about math and would often include funny side comments/jokes every so often to lighten the mood. Very knowledgable. The exams were very tough, but that's not entirely his fault, the course is hard as is whether its Sjamaar teaching or anyone else.

His speaks the way a math book is written. His energy level is quite low. The homework and quizzes are easy, but the exams are unnecessarily hard. He's an excellent mathematician, not a great lecturer.

Great lecturer. Yes, he follows the textbook, but he doesn't regurgitate it - he makes the information more clear and presents examples that aren't in the book. I particularly appreciated that he organized the board in a methodical, easy to follow fashion as he went. Not all math professors are good at this.

mediocre lecturer. just regurgitates the textbook, moves really quickly. grading only depends on the tests, which really depends on which semester u take it. homework takes a while and our prelims were ridiculously hard.

Material and prelims were tough, but his lectures definitely made everything a lot clearer than what it could've been. Very responsible, answers questions quickly on Piazza, and holds extra office hours and all. Amazing professor.
Really difficult class, but Sjamaar is an excellent professor. He does all he can to help his students, and he encourages questions. Definitely would take another class with him.

An amazing professor who explains difficult theoretical concepts with great clarity. He also has a good sense of humor. His teaching reminded me of why I enjoy math so much. The class may be tough but it was worth it and I would love to take another course with this amazing professor again.

Great Professor. I even nominated him as we evaluated the faculty at the end of last semester. Crystal clear; the note on blackboard is extremely good and informative. If you want a solid grasp on linear algebra, I recommend taking his class and don't miss a single class!
No Comments
Excellent professor: very clear, and extremely knowledgeable.  Professors like Sjamaar make Cornell what it is.  Teaches not only the material but also how to think mathematically.
Great class. Very clear presentation of some very difficult concepts. Homeworks were difficult, but covered extremely popular and important ideas and concepts. Really great.
Super engaging lectures, explains complex topics well. Makes proofs a conversation and motivates each step

Really good class but super challenging, def got a lot out of the class. In class quizzes were fairly easy as long as you paid attention in class, and the final was fair. Would recommend starting HWs later as he often makes edits because he realized problems are way too hard.

Professor is amazing, and the workload is light with only 6 homework and 4 small quizzes. Homeworks sometimes require a bit of thinking. Overall, although not too hard, this is a decent course to take if you want to satisfy the probability requirement but don't want to waste credits on trivialities like MATH 4710.

Kleinberg's are amazing and funny. He is extremely good at explaining complex subjects, and will let you see ideas in a new light. The price you pay is that the course is quite difficult and time-consuming. It is definitely worth it though.

Really nice, kind of funny, uber nerdy guy. I find him charming and easy to talk to. Also have him as my faculty advisor. But, hardest class I've ever taken. Could put hours and hours into HW and OH and still get <20% on it, HW/exam medians were about 50%. Maybe worse because it was online. Also I just suck at algo, which is not his fault.

Prof. Kleinberg is one of those professors that, the more complicated the concept, the better he is at explaining it. His lectures on max flow, NP-Completeness, and Turing Machines were very helpful, while I mostly learned about greedy algorithms and DP from the book. His homeworks are definitely very challenging, with HW / Exam medians 50-60%

Prof. Kleinberg is an excellent instructor who is very good at explaining deep concepts, from NP Reductions to Turing Machines to Network Flow problems. His homeworks are quite difficult (some have had medians close to 50%), but I have learned so much in his class.

He basically gives the answers to students he likes and students who have no shame when asking directly for the answers to problem sets. He is also not useful in lecture. The textbook was the only way someone could learn in that class. He just tried to show off and act cool during lecture.
great lecturer. fascinating course material. challenging weekly homework. super useful class.

Bobby is, no lie, a genius and is well-respected. Lectures can be slow, but are very intuitive. #3 on problem sets can be very difficult sometimes. Exams are pretty easy. Advice: start psets the day they get released and rEAD the TEXTBOOK!
reviews.rating_tags: Respected--Get ready to read--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

reviews.comment: He is extremely intelligent and very committed in helping his students learn the material. He can be a little brash when dealing with administrative issues in the course, but seems genuinely interested in his teaching duties. Would highly recommend taking 4820 under him if you want to seriously test your understanding of algorithms.

Professor R. Kleinberg was excellent for Algorithms, Spring 2014. He is very open to student questions and attempts to make a sometimes-challenging subject clear. Not an easy professor by any means, but assessments fair. Relies on the textbook a good amount, but also references outside research.
His lecturing style as well as his mastery of the subject matter are both top-notch. He also seems like a great person. It's an absolute privilege to have him as a lecturer.

Had him for both 4820 and 6820. Great lecturer (he writes a lot, so it is hard sometimes to keep up with both listening and writing down nots). Amazing personality (will go overtime to answer your question). In OH, he will insightfully lead you to think rather than just giving answer away. HW can be super challenging.

R. Kleinberg is an amazing teacher and super smart. His lectures are organized, exciting and the material is super fun and challenging. The HWs are super super challenging. Every HW has a question which is so  hard that sometimes the TAs arent sure on how to solve them. But it is fun to solve the HWS. I really improved my problem solving skills.
Beware........
reviews.rating_tags: Lots of homework--So many papers--Graded by few things

reviews.comment: Dr. Ryan is a great instructor, genuinely cares about his students and is always open to questions. Labs were the highlight of the class.
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Clear grading criteria--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: One of the easiest classes at Cornell if you just show up. Minimal studying is needed if you go to lectures, and pay close attention during labs. Field Trips are engaging and tie back into exams. Lots of small projects, reading, and quizzes throughout the semester so just keep up with all the work. Watch the syllabus and you'll be fine.

The class is pretty easy, lots of different assignment styles, which break up the rigidness unlike other Cornell Science classes. Each lecture is pretty boring, and Prof Ryan is not the most well spoken. There are farm field trips instead of traditional labs that are fun and inspiring. Midterm and Final were both straight forward.

Professor Ryan is a great guy and is very accessible. The TA's were very nice and helpful as well. The class is easy as long as you turn things in on time. The only downside was the 3 hour labs outside on farms during freezing rain or snow.
Fantastic Prof! Made physics fun and engaging. Very cool in class demos + always available to students after class and in office hours. Super reasonable guy- if you need anything just ask him!
Amazing lecturer. Loved taking E&M with it and the grading is very fair.
He is such a cool guy. You can tell he's really passionate about the material. His prelims were fair and the workload was on the lighter side.

Tapping is the kindest and fairest professor you can get at Cornell. Lectures are easy to follow along, and he makes every class enjoyable. You will LOVE this class. It's not too hard to get a B in the class, but DO NOT slack off.

Super sweet and enthusiastic guy. You can genuinely tell teaching is a passion, and he does it well! Always receptive to feedback and willing to help everyone. Class isn't as easy as everyones making it out to be though, its still pretty difficulty, ESPECIALLY when magnetism is introduced. So participate in ur COOPs and stay on top of it :-)

Ryan Tapping is by far my favorite professor at Cornell. He cares so much about all of his students, and is truly enthusiastic about what he teaches. It is easy to do well in his class, because 45% of your grade is participation/homework. The exams are very doable. I hope Cornell recognises professor Tapping for his exceptional teaching.

He is a really nice guy and puts in the effort to make a nice learning environment. He seems to really care about his students and goes out of his way to try to make his lectures more enjoyable. The work is not light though its a physics course so you do get a bunch of homework and everything else that comes with a physics course.
chill dude. A little overrated in my opinion (people act like he is the best prof at the entire school which I don't think is true) but still deserves the five.
The single best professor I've ever had at Cornell. Go to lectures and sections and you'll do well.
Amazing dude, genuinely cares about student feedback and makes needed changes.
Teaches E&M in a very good and easy to understand way, fair exams, and is literally always happy and enthusiastic. the goat of E&M for sure
Goat of physics
Great lectures and honestly just an amazing person as well. He clearly cares about his students and tests are pretty fair. This class is very well taught. OH are very useful as well.

Dr. Tapping is one of the best lecturers I've had at Cornell. He puts a lot of effort into making the lecture material easily understandable and engaging despite it being inherently difficult to understand. I love the jokes and the enthusiasm he brings to each class. Exams are super fair, and lots of practice material is provided beforehand.
reviews.rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Clear grading criteria--Accessible outside class

reviews.comment: Absolutely amazing person and professor. He was always genuinely excited about the content he taught. Now that I am in PHYS2214, I've never appreciated him more. Hes the GOAT.
